title: MATH 3190 Homework 10
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_10 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.

Problem 1 (45 points)
In the itsmr package is the dataset called wine that contains 142 accounts of Australian red wine sales per month from January 1980 to October 1991.

Part a (5 points)
Load in this data and convert it to a tsibble. You can do this by first making it a ts object by typing ts(wine, deltat = 1/12, start = 1980) and then piping that into the as_tsibble() function in the tsibble package.
Then use ggplot() to plot the time series. Show both the points and lines connecting them. On the \(x\) axis, put each year using the scale_x_yearmonth() function and give the plot a title.

wine_tsibble <- ts(wine, deltat = 1/12, start = 1980) |> 
  as_tsibble()

# Plotting the single series
ggplot(wine_tsibble, aes(x = index, y = value)) +
  geom_line(color = "darkred") + 
  geom_point() +
  scale_x_yearmonth(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") + 
  labs(title = "Australian Red Wine Sales", 
       x = "Year", 
       y = "Sales") +
  theme_minimal()










Part b (2 points)
Remove the last 34 rows from the wine data. We will use them to compare how our forecasts look later.

n <- nrow(wine_tsibble)

wine_train <- wine_tsibble[1:(n - 34), ]
wine_test <- wine_tsibble[(n - 33):n, ]



Part c (5 points)
Use the gg_season() plot in the ggtime library to plot the seasonality. Does it seem like there is trend and/or seasonality in this time series? If so, explain the patterns.

gg_season(wine_train, y = value, labels = "both") +
  labs(title = "Seasonal Plot", x = "Date", y = "Sales") +
  theme_minimal()








There appears to be seasonality as each year sales spike over the summer months. There is also evidence of trend where not only is each year over the last, but we can see that the end of a year is always more than the start of that respective year.


Part d (4 points)
Use the adf.test() function in the tseries library to perform a Dickey-Fuller test to determine if we have evidence this time series is stationary. Only the column with the sales should be entered. Since we have data by month, use the k = 12 option. Comment on the results of the test.

adf.test(wine_train$value, k = 12)


    Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test

data:  wine_train$value
Dickey-Fuller = -1.6093, Lag order = 12, p-value = 0.7387
alternative hypothesis: stationary


With 95% confidence, we do not have enough evidence to reject the null hypothesis, meaning we do not have enough evidence to say that the time series is stationary.


Part e (6 points)
Difference the data once. Take \(Y_t = Y_{t+1} - Y_{t}\) for \(t=1,\dots,n-1\). In the notes, I defined the differences as \(Y_t = Y_{t} - Y_{t+1}\), but it actually does not matter. Plot this differenced time series (you can just put the index on the x-axis) and use the adf.test() function again on the differenced data. Again, use k = 12 option. Comment on the results of the test.

wine_diff <- diff(wine_train$value)

plot(wine_diff, type = "l", main = "Differenced Wine Sales", ylab = "Diff Value")







adf.test(wine_diff, k = 12)

Warning in adf.test(wine_diff, k = 12): p-value smaller than printed p-value



    Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test

data:  wine_diff
Dickey-Fuller = -5.0592, Lag order = 12, p-value = 0.01
alternative hypothesis: stationary


When using the differenced value, with 95% confidence, we have enough evidence to reject the null hypothesis, meaning we have enough evidence to say that the time series is stationary.


Part f (4 points)
Now use the auto.arima() function in the forecast library to fit an ARIMA model to this time series (put the entire original tsibble into this function, not the differences time series).
Feel free to check out this resource that discusses more about time series, including what the drift term is.

wine_mod <- auto.arima(wine_train)
wine_mod

Series: wine_train 
ARIMA(1,0,1)(0,1,1)[12] with drift 

Coefficients:
         ar1      ma1     sma1   drift
      0.9144  -0.7519  -0.7247  9.0791
s.e.  0.0918   0.1356   0.1663  1.6526

sigma^2 = 33825:  log likelihood = -639.11
AIC=1288.23   AICc=1288.9   BIC=1301.05




Part g (5 points)
Use the forecast() function from the forecast library to predict the future for 34 months. You should specify you are using the forecast library by typing forecast::forecast() since the itsmr library also has a forecast() function. The input of this function is your model you fit in part (f). Use the model fit to the undifferenced data.
Then use the autoplot() function to plot the forecast.

wine_forecast <- forecast::forecast(wine_mod, level = 95, h = 34)

autoplot(wine_forecast) + 
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1980:1992)

Scale for x is already present.
Adding another scale for x, which will replace the existing scale.











Part h (8 points)
Plot the actual values for 1989, 1990, and 1991 along with the predicted values from the forecast and a shaded 95% interval bands. The geom_ribbon() function may be useful here. Is there any point where the true value is outside the interval? If so, report the year(s) and month(s) where that is the case.

forecast_df <- data.frame(
  time     = as.numeric(time(wine_forecast$mean)),
  forecast = as.numeric(wine_forecast$mean),
  lo95     = as.numeric(wine_forecast$lower[, "95%"]),
  hi95     = as.numeric(wine_forecast$upper[, "95%"])
)

test_df <- data.frame(
  time  = as.numeric(time(as.ts(wine_test))),
  value = as.numeric(as.ts(wine_test))
)

combined_df <- inner_join(forecast_df, test_df, by = "time")

ggplot(combined_df, aes(x = time)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lo95, ymax = hi95, fill = "95% CI"), alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_line(aes(y = forecast, colour = "Forecast")) +
  geom_line(aes(y = value, colour = "Actual")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1989:1992, limits = c(1989, 1992)) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Forecast" = "blue", "Actual" = "red")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("95% CI" = "blue", "Error" = "red")) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Wine Sales", colour = "Lines", fill = "Ribbons") +
  theme_bw()








There is only one point in which the actual value is outside of the 95% confidence interval for the forecast and that is at approximately march of 1991.


Part i (6 points)
Use the checkresiduals() function in the forecast library to create a residual plot, an autocorrelation plot, and a histogram for the residuals. The input to this function should be your model fit with the auto.arima() function. Comment on whether the assumptions seem to be satisfied here. If you’d like, you can make a QQ-plot as well, but the histogram contains the same information.
Note: this function will also give you information from the Ljung-Box test, which tests if the residuals exhibit autocorrelation. A large p-value means we have no evidence of autocorrelation.

checkresiduals(wine_mod)









    Ljung-Box test

data:  Residuals from ARIMA(1,0,1)(0,1,1)[12] with drift
Q* = 19.463, df = 19, p-value = 0.4275

Model df: 3.   Total lags used: 22


The residual plot is showing no obvious patterns. While there is a little fanning occuring, where the variance is unequal, it does not look to be too bad. The autocorrelation plot is not showing any significant autocorrelation, as evidenced by the ljung-box test. The histogram is showing signs of being skewed to the left and having short tails.

title: MATH 3190 Homework 2
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_2 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.

Problem 1 (65 points)
It is time to practice using SQL. First, we will need some files that are worthy of being treated as databases. For this, you will need a little more than 1 GB of hard drive space. Alternatively, you should be able to store these files on your OneDrive. Go ahead and download all of the files from this link: https://suu-my.sharepoint.com/:f:/g/personal/richardbrown1_suu_edu/IgCX2W7pUzK1QIsVpB1dbIF_AQc_ii3Jwm9eHWDBm2xCAyk?e=GeszqA. While the airports.csv and airlines.csv files are quite small, the flights.csv is over 565 MB in size and contains the records of 5,819,079 flights in USA in 2015. These data came from the Bureau of Transportation Statistics. Make sure you save these file outside of your Math_3190_Assignments since you will not be able to push the flights.csv file to GitHub due to its size.

Part a (5 points)Part b (2 points)Part c (1 point)Part d (24 points)Part e (11 points)Part f (10 points)Part g (12 points)


While it is probable that your computer could read in this data set in its entirety, we are going to avoid that and instead make a database that we can access using SQL. Follow the example on slides 55-57 of Notes 4 to create a .sqlite file that contains the tables flights (from flights.csv), airports (from airports.csv), and airlines (from airlines.csv). Again, make sure you save this .sqlite file outside of your Math_3190_Assignments since you will not be able to push it to GitHub due to its size.

library(DBI)
library(RSQLite)

# Create the .sqlite file locally (not in OneDrive)
con <- DBI::dbConnect(RSQLite::SQLite(), "/Users/melaniejensen/Library/CloudStorage/OneDrive-SouthernUtahUniversity/Math_3190/Homework 2 Data/hw2.sqlite") # Edited after assignment to ensure would run properly without creating new sqlite every time the file rendered

# CSVs can still be read from OneDrive
data_path <- "/Users/melaniejensen/Library/CloudStorage/OneDrive-SouthernUtahUniversity/Math_3190/Homework 2 Data"
hw2_files <- list.files(data_path, pattern = "\\.csv$", full.names = FALSE)

for (file in hw2_files) {
  full_file_path <- file.path(data_path, file)
  data_file <- read.csv(full_file_path)
  table_name <- substr(file, 1, nchar(file) - 4)
  dbWriteTable(con, table_name, data_file, overwrite = TRUE)
  rm(data_file)
}

dbListTables(con)



Connect to the .sqlite database that you created in Part a like we did on slide 52 of Notes 5. Then use the dbListTable() function on that connection to list all the tables in the database. There should be three tables that you added in Part a.

con <- DBI::dbConnect(RSQLite::SQLite(), "/Users/melaniejensen/Library/CloudStorage/OneDrive-SouthernUtahUniversity/Math_3190/Homework 2 Data/hw2.sqlite")

DBI::dbListTables(con)

[1] "airlines" "airports" "flights" 




There are many different types of SQL databases: MySQL, SQLite, SQL Server, etc. We created a SQLite database in Part a. A way to see all the columns of a table in an SQLite database is with the command PRAGMA table_info('table_name'). Using your connection from Part b, use the dbGetQuery() function in an R code chunk to print all the column names from the flights table. This is useful when we have a large file that is hard to even open to see what variables are in there.

library(tidyverse)

── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.2.0     ✔ readr     2.2.0
✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
✔ ggplot2   4.0.2     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
✔ lubridate 1.9.5     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
✔ purrr     1.2.1     
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors

dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT * FROM flights") |> head() |> as_tibble()

# A tibble: 6 × 31
   YEAR MONTH   DAY DAY_OF_WEEK AIRLINE FLIGHT_NUMBER TAIL_NUMBER ORIGIN_AIRPORT
  <int> <int> <int>       <int> <chr>           <int> <chr>       <chr>         
1  2015     1     1           4 AS                 98 N407AS      ANC           
2  2015     1     1           4 AA               2336 N3KUAA      LAX           
3  2015     1     1           4 US                840 N171US      SFO           
4  2015     1     1           4 AA                258 N3HYAA      LAX           
5  2015     1     1           4 AS                135 N527AS      SEA           
6  2015     1     1           4 DL                806 N3730B      SFO           
# ℹ 23 more variables: DESTINATION_AIRPORT <chr>, SCHEDULED_DEPARTURE <int>,
#   DEPARTURE_TIME <int>, DEPARTURE_DELAY <int>, TAXI_OUT <int>,
#   WHEELS_OFF <int>, SCHEDULED_TIME <int>, ELAPSED_TIME <int>, AIR_TIME <int>,
#   DISTANCE <int>, WHEELS_ON <int>, TAXI_IN <int>, SCHEDULED_ARRIVAL <int>,
#   ARRIVAL_TIME <int>, ARRIVAL_DELAY <int>, DIVERTED <int>, CANCELLED <int>,
#   CANCELLATION_REASON <chr>, AIR_SYSTEM_DELAY <int>, SECURITY_DELAY <int>,
#   AIRLINE_DELAY <int>, LATE_AIRCRAFT_DELAY <int>, WEATHER_DELAY <int>




Again using the dbGetQuery() function (one query per part) in an R code chunk, use SQL statements to answer the following questions. Note that distances are in miles.

What is the average distance of all flights in the US in 2015?
What is the minimum flight distance of all flights in the US in 2015?
What is the maximum flight distance of all flights in the US in 2015?
Between what two airports did the minimum distance flight take place?
Between what two airports did the maximum distance flight take place? The SELECT DISTINCT command will be useful here.
You might notice that some airports are coded with numbers instead of their IATA codes. Repeat item 5, but do not include the airports that begin with a “1”.
While you may know what these airports codes are, it would be nice if the full name of the airport was printed. The airport code information is in the airport table in your database in the IATA_CODE variable. Use the INNER JOIN statement to output the airport name for the two airports that have the minimum flight distance. Note that this command can take a few seconds to run.
Repeat item 7 with the maximum flight distances.


# 1. 
dbGetQuery(con, "
SELECT AVG(DISTANCE) AS avg_distance
FROM flights
")

  avg_distance
1     822.3565

# 2. 
dbGetQuery(con, "
SELECT MIN(DISTANCE) AS min_distance
FROM flights
")

  min_distance
1           21

# 3. 
dbGetQuery(con, "
SELECT MAX(DISTANCE) AS max_distance
FROM flights
")

  max_distance
1         4983

# 4. 
dbGetQuery(con, "
SELECT DISTINCT ORIGIN_AIRPORT, DESTINATION_AIRPORT
FROM flights
WHERE DISTANCE = (SELECT MIN(DISTANCE) FROM flights)
")

  ORIGIN_AIRPORT DESTINATION_AIRPORT
1            EWR                 JFK

# 5. 
dbGetQuery(con, "
SELECT DISTINCT ORIGIN_AIRPORT, DESTINATION_AIRPORT
FROM flights
WHERE DISTANCE = (SELECT MAX(DISTANCE) FROM flights)
")

  ORIGIN_AIRPORT DESTINATION_AIRPORT
1            JFK                 HNL
2            HNL                 JFK
3          12478               12173
4          12173               12478

# 6. 
dbGetQuery(con, "
SELECT DISTINCT ORIGIN_AIRPORT, DESTINATION_AIRPORT
FROM flights
WHERE DISTANCE = (SELECT MAX(DISTANCE) FROM flights) AND ORIGIN_AIRPORT NOT LIKE '1%' AND DESTINATION_AIRPORT NOT LIKE '1%'
")

  ORIGIN_AIRPORT DESTINATION_AIRPORT
1            JFK                 HNL
2            HNL                 JFK

# 7. 
dbGetQuery(con, "
SELECT ao.AIRPORT AS ORIGIN_FULL_NAME, ad.AIRPORT AS DESTINATION_FULL_NAME
FROM flights as f
INNER JOIN airports as ao ON f.ORIGIN_AIRPORT = ao.IATA_CODE
INNER JOIN airports as ad ON f.DESTINATION_AIRPORT = ad.IATA_CODE
WHERE DISTANCE = (SELECT MIN(DISTANCE) FROM flights)
")

                      ORIGIN_FULL_NAME
1 Newark Liberty International Airport
                                                   DESTINATION_FULL_NAME
1 John F. Kennedy International Airport (New York International Airport)

# 8. 
dbGetQuery(con, "
SELECT DISTINCT ao.AIRPORT AS ORIGIN_FULL_NAME, ad.AIRPORT AS DESTINATION_FULL_NAME
FROM flights as f
INNER JOIN airports as ao ON f.ORIGIN_AIRPORT = ao.IATA_CODE
INNER JOIN airports as ad ON f.DESTINATION_AIRPORT = ad.IATA_CODE
WHERE DISTANCE = (SELECT MAX(DISTANCE) FROM flights)
")

                                                        ORIGIN_FULL_NAME
1 John F. Kennedy International Airport (New York International Airport)
2                                         Honolulu International Airport
                                                   DESTINATION_FULL_NAME
1                                         Honolulu International Airport
2 John F. Kennedy International Airport (New York International Airport)




Many of these queries are generating outputs that are fairly small. That is, they are easily able to be stored as variables in R. A couple hundred thousand rows is fine to work with since that only takes a couple MB of RAM.

Using the dbGetQuery() function again, obtain the arrival delay times and the name of the airline (by joining with the airlines table) for all the data entries that have a delay time more than 60 minutes. Save this output as a tibble in R.


R_tibble <- dbGetQuery(con, "
SELECT a.AIRLINE, f.ARRIVAL_DELAY
FROM flights as f
INNER JOIN airlines AS a ON f.AIRLINE = a.IATA_CODE
WHERE f.ARRIVAL_DELAY > 60
") |> as_tibble()

R_tibble

# A tibble: 319,092 × 2
   AIRLINE                     ARRIVAL_DELAY
   <chr>                               <int>
 1 JetBlue Airways                        85
 2 JetBlue Airways                        89
 3 American Airlines Inc.                102
 4 American Airlines Inc.                 66
 5 United Air Lines Inc.                  78
 6 Delta Air Lines Inc.                  128
 7 American Airlines Inc.                 69
 8 Atlantic Southeast Airlines           226
 9 Skywest Airlines Inc.                 116
10 United Air Lines Inc.                 123
# ℹ 319,082 more rows



Once you have this, make side-by-side boxplots in ggplot() for the arrival delay time split by the airline. This plot won’t look too great unless the y-axis is scaled. Go ahead and use the log10 scale like we did back in Lab 2. Then look up how to angle the axis labels so they do not overlap and incorporate that in your plot.


library(ggplot2)

ggplot(R_tibble, aes(x = AIRLINE, y = log10(ARRIVAL_DELAY))) +
  geom_boxplot(color = 'black', fill = 'lightblue') +
  xlab("Airline") +
  ylab("Scaled Arrival Delay") +
  labs(title = "Arrival Delays Across Airlines") +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1))











Now, let’s NOT use the dbGetQuery() function. Instead, insert an SQL code chunk for this part of the problem that selects the average arrival delay (for some reason, there is not a median function built in to SQL) and the airline’s full name for each airline. So, this output should only have 14 rows.


SELECT a.AIRLINE, AVG(f.ARRIVAL_DELAY)
FROM flights as f
INNER JOIN airlines as a ON f.AIRLINE = a.IATA_CODE
GROUP BY a.AIRLINE;


Displaying records 1 - 10


AIRLINE
AVG(f.ARRIVAL_DELAY)




Alaska Airlines Inc.
-0.9765631


American Airlines Inc.
3.4513721


American Eagle Airlines Inc.
6.4578735


Atlantic Southeast Airlines
6.5853787


Delta Air Lines Inc.
0.1867536


Frontier Airlines Inc.
12.5047064


Hawaiian Airlines Inc.
2.0230928


JetBlue Airways
6.6778608


Skywest Airlines Inc.
5.8456522


Southwest Airlines Co.
4.3749637






Then pass this output back to R and create a barplot with the airlines on the x-axis and the average arrival delay on the y-axis. Again, angle the x-axis labels so they do not overlap.


SELECT a.AIRLINE AS Airline, AVG(f.ARRIVAL_DELAY) AS Avg_Arrival_Delay
FROM flights as f
INNER JOIN airlines as a ON f.AIRLINE = a.IATA_CODE
GROUP BY a.AIRLINE;


airline_arrival_delays <- tibble(airline_arrival_delays)

ggplot(airline_arrival_delays, aes(x = Airline, y = Avg_Arrival_Delay)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", color = 'black', fill = 'lightblue') + 
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1)) +
  xlab("Airline") +
  ylab("Average Arrival Delay") +
  labs(title = "Average Arrival Delays Across Airlines")











Now let’s use the dbplyr add-on to the dplyr package to work with an SQL database directly. You may have to install the dbplyr package. Use the tbl() function to create an object you can work with directly.


con <- DBI::dbConnect(RSQLite::SQLite(), "/Users/melaniejensen/Library/CloudStorage/OneDrive-SouthernUtahUniversity/Math_3190/Homework 2 Data/hw2.sqlite")

library(dbplyr)


Attaching package: 'dbplyr'


The following objects are masked from 'package:dplyr':

    ident, sql

flights_db <- tbl(con, "flights")
airlines_db <- tbl(con, "airlines")


With that object, using R commands from the tidyverse, find the average and standard deviation of the elapsed time of the flights by airline and arrange it by the SD (with the largest at the top). Make sure to use the inner_join() function so that the name of the airline, not just its code, is given. Note that to save this object in R’s memory, you will need to do something like pipe it into the collect() function.


library(tidyverse)

table1 <- flights_db |> 
  inner_join(airlines_db, c("AIRLINE" = "IATA_CODE")) |> 
  group_by(AIRLINE.y) |> 
  summarize(
    Avg_elapsed_time = mean(ELAPSED_TIME, na.rm = TRUE),
    SD_elapsed_time = sd(ELAPSED_TIME, na.rm = TRUE)) |> 
  arrange(desc(SD_elapsed_time)) |> 
  rename(Airline = AIRLINE.y) |> 
  collect()


Finally, make a ggplot bar graph like the one you made in Part f, but use the standard deviation as the heights on the y-axis. Why do you suppose the airline with the largest standard deviation in elapsed time has such variable flight times?


ggplot(table1, aes(x = reorder(Airline, -SD_elapsed_time), y = SD_elapsed_time)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", color = 'black', fill = 'lightblue') + 
  theme_bw() + 
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1)) +
  xlab("Airline") +
  ylab("Standard Deviation of Elapsed Time") +
  labs(title = "Standard Deviation of Elapsed Time Across Airlines")











Finally, when you are done with this assignment, it is good practice to close the connection to the flights database.

dbDisconnect(con)

title: MATH 3190 Homework 7
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. library(umap)
library(math3190package)


Problem 1 (57 points)
In Homework 6, we briefly looked at a small sample from this data set. Now we have the full data set with 20,640 districts. For districts in California from 1990, we want to predict the median house value for the district based on the district location (longitude and latitude), the median house age in the block group, the total number of rooms in the homes, the total number of bedrooms, the population in the district, the number of households, and the median income (in $10,000). These data were obtained from scikit-learn.org and can be found in the Data folder of the Math3190_Sp26 GitHub repo with the name cal_housing.data.

Part a (5 points)
Read in the data. The read.csv() function works for files that end in .data if they are comma separated like this file is. However, be aware that the housing data does not have column names, so make sure to put header = FALSE. Assign appropriate column names to the columns and then take logs (base e) of all variables except lat and long. Comparing the two models, the PLSR model explains variation in log median house value more efficiently — reaching near its maximum R² of 67.57% in fewer components than the PCR model. This is expected, as PLSR constructs components by maximizing covariance with the outcome, whereas PCR constructs components solely based on variation in the predictors without regard to the outcome.
Conversely, the PCR model captures variation in the predictors more efficiently in the first few components, where 4 components in the PLSR explains ~91% compared to the PCR model’s explained ~97% of predictor variance. This tradeoff reflects the fundamental difference between the two methods — PCR prioritizes explaining the predictors while PLSR prioritizes explaining the outcome.
In this model, a ‘good’ number of components is 5 components as the difference in marginal variation in loged median house value is smaller between 5-6 components than it was 4-5.


Part h (8 points)
In part (e), you said how many components were needed for the PCA to explain a good amount of the \(Y\) variable. Using that number of components, use the PCA and PLS models to find the root MSE when predicting the testing set. Note: the root MSE (or residual standard error, also abbreviated RMSE) is found by taking the square root of the sum of squared residuals divided by the residual degrees of freedom, which is \(n-p-1\), where \(p\) is the number of predictors in the model. Also, be aware that when you use the predict() function on your model, it will give you predictions for each number of components. You can access the predictions for using 3 components, for example, by typing predict(pca_model, test_set)[,,3].

plsr_rmse

The PLSR model outperformed the PCR model in terms of predictive accuracy, achieving a lower test RMSE of 0.362 compared to 0.409 for PCR (in log units). This is consistent with the summary output, as PLSR explained a greater proportion of variation in log median house value with fewer components, reflecting its advantage of directly optimizing for the outcome variable during component construction.

title: MATH 3190 Homework 4
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_4 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.
Some of the parts in this homework, like 1a, 2b, 2c, 3a, and 3b require writing down some math-heavy expressions. You may either type it up using LaTeX-style formatting in Quarto (preferred), or you can write it by hand (neatly) and include pictures or scans of your work in your Quarto document.

Problem 1 (33 points)
Suppose we want to find the value of \(x\) and the objection function value for finding the global maximum and minimum of the function \(f(x)=\ln(1+x^2)(1+x)e^{-x^2}\). We will implement gradient descent and Newton’s method for finding these extrema.

Part a (5 points)
Find \(f'(x)\) for this function. Do this by hand as a nice derivative refresher. You don’t need to include all of your work, though. Feel free to check your answer using something like Wolfram|Alpha. Write down the derivative here.
\[
f(x) = e^{-x^2}ln(1+x^2) + xe^{-x^2}ln(1+x^2)
\]

\(e^{-x^2}ln(1+x^2)\)
\(\begin{alignedat}{2}
u &= e^{-x^2} & \qquad du &= -2xe^{-x^2} \\
v &= ln(1+x^2) & \qquad dv &= \frac{2x}{1+x^2} \\
\end{alignedat}\)
\(xe^{-x^2}ln(1+x^2)\)
\(\begin{alignedat}{2}
u &= xe^{-x^2} & \qquad du &= (1-2x^2)e^{-x^2} \\
v &= ln(1+x^2) & \qquad dv &= \frac{2x}{x^2+1} \\
\end{alignedat}\)

\[
f'(x) = (e^{-x^2})(\frac{2x}{1+x^2}) + (-2xe^{-x^2})(ln(1+x^2)) + (xe^{-x^2})(\frac{2x}{x^2+1}) + ((1-2x^2)e^{-x^2})(ln(1+x^2))
\]
\[
f'(x) = (-2x^2 - 2x + 1)e^{-x^2}ln(1+x^2) + \frac{2x(1+x)e^{-x^2}}{1+x^2}
\]


Part b (4 points)
Below is the code of a function that implements gradient descent using a backtracking line search each iteration to find \(\gamma_k\): the step size. This function takes eight inputs:

The starting value (or vector)
The function to optimize
The function’s derivative (or gradient)
A logical indicating if we want to find a max with a default of FALSE.
The maximum number of iterations with a default of 200
The initial step size with a default of 1
The line search beta parameter with a default of 0.5.
The stopping tolerance with a default of 1e-10.

The output of this function is a list with the \(x\) value (or vector) that optimizes the function, the function value at that point, and the number of iterations it took to converge. This function works in the case of one dimension or multiple dimensions since you’ll use it again in problem 2.
Take a few minutes to study this function to make sure you understand how it works and what it is doing. Write a few sentences explaining how this function works.

gradient_desc <- function(starting, f, gradient, max = F, maxit = 200, step_size = 1, line_parameter = 0.5, tol = 1e-10) {

  if(max == T) {
    obj_func <- function(x) {
      -f(x)
    }
    func_deriv <- function(x) {
      -gradient(x)
    }
  } else {
    obj_func <- f
    func_deriv <- gradient
  }
  xk <- starting
  for(k in 1:maxit) {
    gamma <- step_size
    line_beta <- line_parameter
    # Below is the backtracking line search
    while(obj_func(xk - gamma * func_deriv(xk)) > 
          obj_func(xk) - gamma/4 * sum(func_deriv(xk)^2)) {
      gamma <- line_beta * gamma
    }
    # Once we have a good step size, continue
    xnew <- xk - gamma * func_deriv(xk)
    if(norm(xk - xnew, type = "2") < tol) {
      break
    }
    xk <- xnew
    }
  return(list(x = xk, function_val = f(xk), iters = k))
}

First, the function determines whether the user wants to minimize or maximize the objective function. If the user wants to maximize the objective function, the function will minimize the negative objective function. Then, the function performs backtracking line search. This algorithm starts with an initial step size and then checks if the function value at the new point (after taking a step in the direction of the negative gradient) is sufficiently smaller than the function value at the current point. If it is not, the step size is reduced by multiplying it by a line parameter (which is between 0 and 1). This process continues until a suitable step size is found. After finding a good step size, the function updates the current point and checks for convergence by comparing the distance between the new point and the current point to a specified tolerance. If convergence is achieved, the loop breaks, and the function returns the optimal point, the function value at that point, and the number of iterations taken to converge.

Give the likelihood \(L(\theta|\boldsymbol{x})\) and log-likelihood \(\ell(\theta|\boldsymbol{x})\) functions in this situation.
Likelihood Function
\[
L(\theta|\boldsymbol{x}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n}\theta(1-x_i)^{\theta-1} \\
L(\theta|\boldsymbol{x}) = \theta^{n}\prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-x_i)^{\theta-1}

Log-Likelihood Function
\[

\]
\[
\ell(\theta|\boldsymbol{x}) = ln(\theta^{n}) + ln(\prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-x_i)^{\theta-1}) \\
\]
\[
\ell(\theta|\boldsymbol{x}) = ln(\theta^{n}) + \sum_{i=1}^{n}ln((1-x_i)^{\theta-1}) \\
\]
\[
\ell(\theta|\boldsymbol{x}) = nln(\theta) + \sum_{i=1}^{n}(\theta-1)ln(1-x_i) \\
\]
\[
\ell(\theta|\boldsymbol{x}) = nln(\theta) + (\theta-1)\sum_{i=1}^{n}ln(1-x_i) \\
\]


Part c (5 points)
Find the Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE) \(\hat{\theta}\) for \(\theta\).
\[
\frac{d\ell}{d\theta} = \frac{n}{\theta} + \sum_{i=1}^{n}ln(1-x_i) = 0 \\
\frac{n}{\theta} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n}ln(1-x_i) \\
\hat{\theta} = -\frac{n}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}ln(1-x_i)}
\]


Part d (3 points)
Show that your estimator is in fact a maximum. That is, check the second derivative of the log-likelihood.
\[
\frac{d^{2}\ell}{d\theta^{2}} = -\frac{n}{\theta^{2}}
\]
Since \(n\) and \(\theta^2\) are both positive, \(\frac{d^{2}\ell}{d\theta^{2}} < 0\) for all \(\theta \in (0, \infty)\). Because the log-likelihood is strictly concave over its entire domain, the unique critical point \(\hat{\theta}\) is the global maximum.

Problem 3 (31 points)
We implemented optimization to maximize the likelihood for a Poisson regression problem in the notes to estimate the \(\beta\) (intercept and slope) values. Let’s do something similar for find the \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) vector by maximizing the likelihood in logistic regression.
In logistic regression, we attempt to predict the probability of a success for a given case. We can use the Bernoulli random variable for this. For a Bernoulli random variable, \(P(Y=y_i) = p_i^{y_i}(1-p_i)^{1-y_i}\) for \(y_i=0,1\) where \(p_i\) is the probability of a success. In our case, for logistic regression, we have \(\text{logit}(p_i)=\ln\left(\dfrac{p_i}{1-p_i}\right)=\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta}\). That means \(p_i = \dfrac{\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})}{1+\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})}\).
The likelihood function, \(L(\boldsymbol{\beta}|\mathbf{X},\boldsymbol{y})\), for the logistic regression for a sample of size \(n\) is \[
\begin{align*}
L(\boldsymbol{\beta}|\mathbf{X},\boldsymbol{y}) &=\prod_{i=1}^n p_i^{y_i}(1-p_i)^{1-y_i}\\
&=\prod_{i=1}^n\left[\dfrac{\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})}{1+\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})}\right]^{y_i}\left(1-\left[\dfrac{\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})}{1+\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})}\right]\right)^{1-y_i}
\end{align*}
\]

\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}|\mathbf{X},\boldsymbol{y})=\sum_{i=1}^ny_i\ln\left(\dfrac{\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})}{1+\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})}\right) + \sum_{i=1}^n(1-y_i)\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{1+\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})}\right)
\] and then show it can be equivalently written \[

\] using properties of logarithms.
\[
L(\boldsymbol{\beta}|\mathbf{X},\boldsymbol{y}) = \prod_{i=1}^n\left[\dfrac{\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})}{1+\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})}\right]^{y_i}\left(1-\left[\dfrac{\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})}{1+\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})}\right]\right)^{1-y_i} \\

\[
\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}|\mathbf{X},\boldsymbol{y}) =
\sum_{i=1}^ny_i\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta} - y_i\ln\left[1+\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})\right] + 0 - \ln\left[1+\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})\right] - (0 - y_i\ln\left[1+\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})\right])) \\
\]
\[
\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}|\mathbf{X},\boldsymbol{y}) =
\sum_{i=1}^ny_i\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta} - y_i\ln\left[1+\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})\right] - \ln\left[1+\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})\right] + y_i\ln\left[1+\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})\right]\\
\]
\[
\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}|\mathbf{X},\boldsymbol{y}) =
\sum_{i=1}^ny_i\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta} - \ln\left[1+\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})\right]
\]
\[
\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}|\mathbf{X},\boldsymbol{y}) =
\sum_{i=1}^ny_i\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta} - \sum_{i=1}^n\ln\left[1+\exp(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta})\right] \\
\]


Part b (6 points)
Find the gradient of the log likelihood if we have three \(\beta\)’s. That is, if \(\mathbf{X}_i\boldsymbol{\beta}=\beta_0+\beta_1x_{i1}+\beta_2x_{i2}\).
\[
\frac{d\ell}{d\boldsymbol{\beta}_0} =
\sum_{i=1}^n\left[y_i - \frac{\exp(\boldsymbol{\beta}_0 + \boldsymbol{\beta}_1\mathbf{X}_{i1} + \boldsymbol{\beta}_2\mathbf{X}_{i2})}{1+\exp(\boldsymbol{\beta}_0 + \boldsymbol{\beta}_1\mathbf{X}_{i1} + \boldsymbol{\beta}_2\mathbf{X}_{i2})}\right] =
\sum_{i=1}^n\left[y_i-p_i\right] \\
\]
\[
\frac{d\ell}{d\boldsymbol{\beta}_1} =
\sum_{i=1}^n\left[y_i\mathbf{X}_{i1} -\frac{\exp(\boldsymbol{\beta}_0 + \boldsymbol{\beta}_1\mathbf{X}_{i1} + \boldsymbol{\beta}_2\mathbf{X}_{i2})}{1+\exp(\boldsymbol{\beta}_0 + \boldsymbol{\beta}_1\mathbf{X}_{i1} + \boldsymbol{\beta}_2\mathbf{X}_{i2})}\right] = \sum_{i=1`}^n\left[y_i\mathbf{X}_{i1} - p_i\mathbf{X}_{i1}\right] = \sum_{i=1}^n\left[\mathbf{X}_{i1}(y_i-p_i)\right] \\
\]
\[
\frac{d\ell}{d\boldsymbol{\beta}_2} =
\sum_{i=1}^n\left[y_i\mathbf{X}_{i2} -\frac{\exp(\boldsymbol{\beta}_0 + \boldsymbol{\beta}_1\mathbf{X}_{i1} + \boldsymbol{\beta}_2\mathbf{X}_{i2})}{1+\exp(\boldsymbol{\beta}_0 + \boldsymbol{\beta}_1\mathbf{X}_{i1} + \boldsymbol{\beta}_2\mathbf{X}_{i2})}\right] = \sum_{i=1`}^n\left[y_i\mathbf{X}_{i2} - p_i\mathbf{X}_{i2}\right] = \sum_{i=1}^n\left[\mathbf{X}_{i2}(y_i-p_i)\right]
\]


Part c (4 points)
In Homework 3, Problem 2, part a, you read in the adult dataset (from the UC Irvine database). This is the one that we used to predict whether a person makes over $50K a year based on some other variables. The data came from the Census Bureau in 1994 and can be found in the Data folder in my Math3190_S26 GitHub repo. More info on the dataset can be found in the “adult.names” file.
Read in the dataset and put column names like you did in HW 3. Then fit a logistic regression model using the glm() function that predicts salary from age/10 and sex. Note, we should divide the age by 10 in our model because this makes the gradient descent more stable (for whatever reason). We can divide by 10 in the glm() function by wrapping it in the I() function. Like this: glm(salary ~ I(age/10) + sex, data = adult, family = binomial).

Define y, x1, and x2. y should be a vector of 0’s and 1’s with a 1 indicating that the person had a salary above $50,000. x1 should be a vector that contains all of the age values (divided by 10) and then x2 should be an indicator variable that indicates if the person is male or not. Part e (7 points)
Use the gradient descent function from problem 1 to find estimates for \(\beta_0\), \(\beta_1\), and \(\beta_2\) for predicting salary from age and sex. Enter the following in your function:

Use the vector c(0, 0, 0) as your starting point.
Instead of starting the step size, \(\gamma_k\), at 1, start it at 0.1 to save some time with the line search.
Make sure the maximum number of iterations is at least 200.

You’ll know you did this right if the optimized values you end up with match (or are very close to) the estimates from your logistic regression model you fit in part c. This may take a minute or two to run, so you may want to cache this code chunk.

f <- function(beta, y, x_long) {
  n <- length(y)
  X <- cbind(1, x_long[1:n], x_long[(n+1):(2*n)])
  z <- X %*% beta
  val <- sum(y * z - log(1 + exp(z)))
  
  return(as.vector(val))
}

f_prime <- function(beta, y, x_long) {
  n <- length(y)
  X <- cbind(1, x_long[1:n], x_long[(n+1):(2*n)])
  z <- X %*% beta
  p <- exp(z) / (1 + exp(z))
  grad <- t(X) %*% (y - p)
  return(as.vector(grad))
}

gradient_desc(starting = c(0, 0, 0), f = function(b) f(b, y, c(x1,x2)), 
  gradient = function(b) f_prime(b, y, c(x1,x2)), max = TRUE, maxit = 500, 
  step_size = 0.1)

title: MATH 3190 Homework 8
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_8 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.

Problem 1 (10 points)
Three airlines serve a small town in Ohio. Airline A has 52% of all scheduled flights, airline B has 35% and airline C has the remaining 13%. Their on-time rates are 85%, 67%, and 41%, respectively. A flight just left on-time. What is the probability that it was a flight of airline A?

# P(A | on-time) = ( P(on-time | A) * P(A) ) / P(on-time)

print((0.85 * 0.52) / (0.85 * 0.52 + 0.67 * 0.35 + 0.41 * 0.13))

[1] 0.6056454




Problem 2 (15 points)
Suppose we have a data set with each observation \(x_i\) independent and identically exponentially distributed for \(i=1,2,\dots,n\). That is, \(x_i\sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)\) where \(\lambda\) is the rate parameter. We would like to find a posterior (or at least a function proportional to it) for \(\lambda\).

Part a (6 points)
Write down the likelihood function (or a function proportional to it) in this situation. We would call this \(p(x|\lambda)\).
\[p(x | \lambda) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \lambda e^{-\lambda x_i} = \lambda^n \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda x_i} = \lambda^n e^{-\lambda \sum_{i=1}^n x_i}\]


Part b (6 points)
Now let \(\lambda\) have a normal prior with mean 0.1 and variance 1: \(\lambda\sim N(1/10,\ 1)\). Use this and the likelihood from part (a) to write down a function that is proportional to the posterior of \(\lambda\) given \({x}\). We call this \(p(\lambda|{x})\).
\[p(\lambda \mid x) \propto \lambda^n \exp\!\left\{-\lambda \sum_{i=1}^n x_i - \frac{(\lambda - 0.1)^2}{2}\right\}\]


Part c (3 points)
Which would be more appropriate here to obtain samples of \(\lambda\), the Gibbs or Metropolis algorithm? Explain why. You may want to look on page 171 of Notes 9 in the conjugate prior table.
It would be more appropriate to use the Metropolis algorithm here since the posterior of \(\lambda\) does not have a known distribution that we can easily sample from using the Gibbs algorithm. Additionally, as we know the function proportional to the posterior, we can use the Metropolis algorithm to sample from it much more easily.



Problem 3 (30 points)
Suppose we have the vector x = c(1.83, 1.72, 2.13, 2.49, 0.90, 2.01, 1.51, 3.12, 1.29, 1.54, 2.94, 3.02, 0.93, 2.78) that we believe comes from a gamma distribution with shape of 10 and some rate \(\beta\): \(x_i\sim\text{Gam}(10,\ \beta)\). We will use sampling to obtain some information about \(\beta\). Let’s put a gamma prior on \(\beta\) with a shape of \(\alpha_0\) and a rate of 1: \(\beta\sim\text{Gam}(\alpha_0,\ 1)\).

Part a (5 points)
Use the fact that this is a conjugate prior to write down what kind of distribution the posterior of \(\beta\), which is \(p(\beta|{x})\), is.
\(x_i \sim \text{Gam}(10, \beta)\)
\[f(x_i \mid \beta) = \frac{\beta^{10}}{\Gamma(10)} x_i^{9} e^{-\beta x_i}\]
Likelihood:
\[p({x} \mid \beta) = \prod_{i=1}^n \frac{\beta^{10}}{\Gamma(10)} x_i^{9} e^{-\beta x_i} = \frac{\beta^{10n}}{\Gamma(10)^n} \left(\prod_{i=1}^n x_i\right)^9 e^{-\beta \left(\sum_{i=1}^n x_i\right)}\]
\[p({x} \mid \beta) \propto \beta^{10n} e^{-\beta \left(\sum_{i=1}^n x_i\right)}\]
Prior:
\[p(\beta) = \frac{1^{\alpha_0}}{\Gamma(\alpha_0)} \beta^{\alpha_0 - 1} e^{-\beta}\]
\[p(\beta) \propto \beta^{\alpha_0 - 1} e^{-\beta}\]
Posterior = Likelihood * Prior:
\[p(\beta \mid {x}) \propto p({x} \mid \beta) \cdot p(\beta)\]
\[p(\beta \mid {x}) \propto \left[\beta^{10n} e^{-\beta \left(\sum_{i=1}^n x_i\right)}\right] \cdot \left[\beta^{\alpha_0 - 1} e^{-\beta}\right]\]
\[p(\beta \mid {x}) \propto \beta^{10n + \alpha_0 - 1} \cdot e^{-\beta\left(\left(\sum_{i=1}^n x_i\right) + 1\right)}\]
\[p(\beta \mid {x}) = \text{Gam}\!\left(10n + \alpha_0,\ \left(\sum_{i=1}^n x_i\right) + 1\right)\]


Part b (5 points)
Let \(\alpha_0=1\). In an R code chunk, sample 10,000 \(\beta\) values from the distribution you wrote down in part (a) using the rgamma() function and report the 95% credible interval for \(\beta\) using the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles.

x = c(1.83, 1.72, 2.13, 2.49, 0.90, 2.01, 1.51, 3.12, 1.29, 1.54, 2.94, 3.02, 0.93, 2.78)
alpha_0 <- 1

set.seed(2026)
sample_betas <- rgamma(10000, shape = 10 * length(x) + alpha_0, rate = sum(x) + 1)

quantile(sample_betas, probs = c(0.025, 0.975))

4.057098 5.644936

Part e (4 points)
Comment on the effect the value of \(\alpha_0\) has on the credible intervals for \(\beta\) as revealed in parts (b), (c), and (d).

print(5.644936 - 4.057098)

[1] 1.587838

print(5.977442 - 4.340462)

[1] 1.63698

print(9.271471 - 7.204932)

[1] 2.066539


As alpha_0 increases, the intervals not only shift to the right, but they are also getting wider. This makes sense because as alpha_0 increases, the shape parameter and the variance of the prior distribution for beta are increasing, which puts more weight on larger values of betas while also increasing the variance of the posterior distribution for beta. This results in wider posterior probability intervals for beta as we say in the random sample outputs above.


Part f (6 points)
Now suppose we have twice as much data (given in the R code chunk below). Part g (4 points)
In this problem, the true \(\beta\) value is 5. Write a sentence or two about the effect adding more data has to these credible intervals by comparing the intervals from parts (b)-(d) to the intervals from part (f).

print(5.729326 - 4.538663)

[1] 1.190663

print(5.902571 - 4.693303)

[1] 1.209268

print(7.630896 - 6.246754)

[1] 1.384142


We can see for all of the intervals, adding more data narrowed the posterior probability intervals. While adding more data also reduced how much the intervals shift to the right, we can see that when alpha_0 is increased up to 100, both intervals still fail to capture the true beta of 5, with the interval found in part f (more data) being closer to capturing the true beta than the interval found in part d. 



Problem 4 (35 points)
Let’s apply the Bayesian framework to a regression problem. In the math3190package library is the treeseeds dataset (it is also on GitHub) that contains information about species of tree, the count of seeds it produces, and the average weight of those seeds in mg.

Part a (3 points)
Using the treeseeds data, create new variables defined by taking the log of the counts and weights. Fit an OLS regression model using logged weight to predict logged count.

library(math3190package) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
tree <- treeseeds |>
  mutate(lcount = log(count),
         lweight = log(weight))

ols_model <- lm(lcount ~ lweight, data = tree)
summary(ols_model)

(Intercept)  9.37903    0.39720  23.613 1.95e-14 ***
lweight     -0.51491    0.07185  -7.166 1.58e-06 ***
---
