Residual standard error: 0.9363 on 17 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.7513,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7367 
F-statistic: 51.35 on 1 and 17 DF,  p-value: 1.58e-06




Part b (5 points)
Please read this carefully and try to understand what is being done here. There is just one question at the end of this part for you. We will walk through the mathematics and code of obtaining the posterior and samples together here since this problem will focus on looking at diagnostics, but I want you to see the Metropolis algorithm in action! Make sure to read this in the html or pdf file where all equations are rendered nicely.
Assuming the true errors are normal with mean 0 and variance \(\sigma^2\), \(\epsilon_i\sim N(0,\ \sigma^2)\), it can be shown that each \(y_i\) has the distribution \[
p(y_i|x_i,\beta_0,\beta_1\sigma^2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\exp\left(
-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}(y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1x_i)^2\right)
\]
So, we can write the likelihood is \[
p(y_i|x_i,\beta_0,\beta_1\sigma^2)\propto\exp\left(
-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}(y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1x_i)^2\right)
\] where \(y_i\) is the log(count) for observation \(i\) and \(x_i\) is the log(weight) for observation \(i\). Note that here we think of \(\boldsymbol{y}\) as being random and \({x}\) as being fixed. We could, in theory, think of the vector \({x}\) as also being random and put a prior on it. But we won’t do that here.
Now, let’s just put uniform priors on \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) so the priors are proportional to 1. Also, let’s assume \(\sigma^2=1\). This seems reasonable since \(s_e^2\), the MSE, is 0.877. Of course, we could put a prior on \(\sigma^2\) as well and sample it too, but we will focus on only sampling \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\).
Now, with those uniform priors, and plugging in 1 for \(\sigma^2\), we have that the joint posterior of \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) is: \[
p(\beta_0,\beta_1|{x},\boldsymbol{y}) \propto\exp\left(
-\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1x_i)^2\right)=f(\beta_0,\beta_1|{x},\boldsymbol{y}).
\]
Then, we can take the log to get \[
\ln(f(\beta_0,\beta_1|{x},\boldsymbol{y}))=-\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1x_i)^2.
\]
Our goal now is to obtain samples of \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\). Let’s use the Metropolis algorithm to do this. Using the log of the function proportional to the joint posterior of \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\), \(\ln\Big(f(\beta_0,\beta_1|{x},\boldsymbol{y})\Big)\), we’ll write a Metropolis algorithm in R. For \(\beta_0\), we will use a normal proposal distribution centered at the previous value, \(\beta_0^{(i)}\), with a standard deviation of 0.8 and for \(\beta_1\), and we will use a normal proposal distribution centered at the previous value, \(\beta_1^{(i)}\), with a standard deviation of 0.1. The starting values don’t matter too much, but we’ll use \(\beta_0^{(0)}=10\) and \(\beta_1^{(0)}=-0.5\).
This code will generate 10,000 samples and plot the chains for \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\). In addition, the 95% credible interval for \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) are given.

library(patchwork)
set.seed(2026)
nsamps <- 10000
beta0 <- rep(0, nsamps)
beta1 <- rep(0, nsamps)
beta0[1] <- 10
beta1[1] <- -0.5
for (i in 1:(nsamps - 1)) {
  beta0_star <- rnorm(1, beta0[i], 0.8) # Proposed new value for intercept
  beta1_star <- rnorm(1, beta1[i], 0.1) # Proposed new value for slope
  # y is tree$lcount and x is tree$lweight
  lf1 <- -1 / 2 * sum((tree$lcount - beta0_star - beta1_star * tree$lweight)^2)
  lf2 <- -1 / 2 * sum((tree$lcount - beta0[i] - beta1[i] * tree$lweight)^2)
  if (log(runif(1)) < lf1 - lf2) {
    # Accept the proposed values as new sample values if the above 
    #   condition is satisfied
    beta0[i + 1] <- beta0_star
    beta1[i + 1] <- beta1_star
  } else {
    # Otherwise set the new sample values to be equal to the previous ones
    beta0[i + 1] <- beta0[i]
    beta1[i + 1] <- beta1[i]
  }
}

mcmc_df <- data.frame(index = 1:nsamps, beta0 = beta0, beta1 = beta1)
mcmc_plot1 <- 
  ggplot(mcmc_df, aes(x = index, y = beta0)) +
  geom_line() +
  theme_test() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 10000, by = 2000)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(8, 11, by = 0.5)) + 
  labs(x = "Index", y = expression(beta[0]))

mcmc_plot2 <- 
  ggplot(mcmc_df, aes(x = index, y = beta1)) +
  geom_line() +
  theme_test() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 10000, by = 2000)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(-0.75, -0.25, by = 0.1)) + 
  labs(x = "Index", y = expression(beta[1]))

mcmc_plot1 / mcmc_plot2







quantile(beta0, c(.025, .975))

     2.5%     97.5% 
 8.526416 10.210557 

quantile(beta1, c(.025, .975))

      2.5%      97.5% 
-0.6606829 -0.3681291 


The plots of the chains are given in the output above.
The 95% credible interval for \(\beta_0\) is \((8.53, 10.21)\).
The 95% credible interval for \(\beta_1\) is \((-0.661, -0.368)\).
Your intervals may differ a bit due to the random sampling.
Okay, here is the question for you! Based on the plots of the chains above, does it look like the Metropolis sampling worked fairly well? Explain.
It looks like the Metropolis sampling worked fairly well as the sampling chains for both \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) do not appear to be too slow or get stuck in place for too long, which indicates that the proposed distributions for both \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) fit the target distribution well.


Part c (3 points)
Interpret both of the credible intervals from part (b).
There is a 95% posterior probability that the true value of \(\beta_0\) is between 8.5264 and 10.2106.
There is a 95% posterior probability that the true value of \(\beta_1\) is between -0.6607 and -0.3681.


Part d (5 points)
Find and report the integrated autocorrelation time for the \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) chains. Each chain will have their own \(\hat{\tau}_{int}\) value, so you should report two (although they will be similar).

tau_beta0 <- 1 + 2 * sum(abs(acf(beta0, lag.max = 100, plot = FALSE)$acf))
tau_beta1 <- 1 + 2 * sum(abs(acf(beta1, lag.max = 100, plot = FALSE)$acf))

tau_beta0

[1] 25.73468

tau_beta1

[1] 26.61679




Part e (3 points)
Based on the integrated autocorrelation time for the \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) chains, how many MCMC samples would you need to generate to get the equivalent of 10,000 independent samples?
The integrated autocorrelation of \(\beta_0\) is \(\hat{\tau}_{int} = 25.73\) and for \(\beta_1\) is \(\hat{\tau}_{int} = 26.62\). This means that approximately every 27 samples, we obtain one effectively independent sample. To get the equivalent of 10,000 independent samples, we would need to generate approximately 266,617 samples.


Part f (3 points)
Let’s compare these credible intervals to some other intervals. First, obtain the 95% \(t\) confidence intervals for \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) just using the confint() function on the model you fit in part (a) and report them here.

confint(ols_model, level = 0.95)

                 2.5 %     97.5 %
(Intercept)  8.5410148 10.2170375
lweight     -0.6665051 -0.3633051




Part g (8 points)
Now let’s obtain confidence intervals using bootstrapping the cases like we did with in Notes 7 and HW 5. You can use the bootstrapping() function in the math3190package for this or do it yourself with a loop. Set a seed and then using at least 10,000 bootstrap samples, report the 95% percentile confidence intervals for \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\).

coef_func <- function(data, i) {
  fit <- lm(lcount ~ lweight, data = data[i, ])
  coef(fit)
}

set.seed(2026)
boot_out <- bootstrapping(tree, coef_func, 10000)

# Beta 0
quantile(boot_out$t[, 1], c(0.025, 0.975))

     2.5%     97.5% 
 8.396988 10.289586 

# Beta 1
quantile(boot_out$t[, 2], c(0.025, 0.975))

      2.5%      97.5% 
-0.6671630 -0.3496652 




Part i (5 points)
Write a couple sentences comparing all of the intervals in parts (b), (f), and (g).

library(knitr)

interval_table <- data.frame(
  Method = c("OLS confint()", "Metropolis", "Bootstrap"),
  Beta0_2.5 = c(8.53, 8.53, 8.396988),
  Beta0_97.5 = c(10.21, 10.21, 10.289586),
  Beta1_2.5 = c(-0.661, -0.661, -0.6671630),
  Beta1_97.5 = c(-0.368, -0.368, -0.3496652)
)

kable(interval_table, 
      col.names = c("Method", "$\\beta_0$ 2.5%", "$\\beta_0$ 97.5%", 
                    "$\\beta_1$ 2.5%", "$\\beta_1$ 97.5%"),
      digits = 4,
      caption = "95% Confidence/Credible Intervals for $\\beta_0$ and $\\beta_1$")


95% Confidence/Credible Intervals for \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\)









Method
\(\beta_0\) 2.5%
\(\beta_0\) 97.5%
\(\beta_1\) 2.5%
\(\beta_1\) 97.5%




OLS confint()
8.530
10.2100
-0.6610
-0.3680


Metropolis
8.530
10.2100
-0.6610
-0.3680


Bootstrap
8.397
10.2896
-0.6672
-0.3497





We can see that across the board all of the intervals are very similar. This shows that all of the methods are identifying the same information about the parameters. This indicates that the prior on the Metropolis sampling did not have a large influence on the posterior, which makes the likelihood the main driver. This is why the Metropolis and the OLS intervals are basically the same. The bootstrap intervals, while still being very similar, are a bit wider than the other two methods, which is likely due to the fact that there are not as many assumptions being made with bootstrapping as there are with the other two methods, which leads to more uncertainty in the estimates and thus wider intervals.

title: MATH 3190 Homework 9
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_9 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.

Problem 1 (70 points)
Before we begin, please re-install the math3190package since there was an update to the classifier_plot() function that is needed on this assignment. Use either devtools::install_github("rbrown53/math3190package") or
remotes::install_github("rbrown53/math3190package").

remotes::install_github("rbrown53/math3190package")
library(math3190package)
library(faraway)
library(rsample)
library(caret)
library(tidyverse)
library(naivebayes)

The National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases conducted a study on 768 adult female Pima Native Americans living near Phoenix. The purpose of the study was to investigate factors related to diabetes. The response variable in this study (test) was whether or not the patient showed signs of diabetes (1 = yes, 0 = no). One explanatory variable considered was the plasma glucose concentration (in mg/dL) at 2 hours in an oral glucose tolerance test (glucose). Many other potential explanatory variables were considered as well including body mass index (BMI). The goal here is to predict the diabetes status of the subject, which is in the results variable.

Part a (5 points)
Install and/or load the faraway package with the pima dataset. Set a seed and then split the data into a testing and a training set with the testing set containing 20% of the data values. When you do this, change the name of the variable “test” to “result” since it will be a bit confusing to call that test when we have a test dataset. Then make the result variable a factor so R treats it as categorical rather than numeric.

pima_data <- faraway::pima

pima_data <- pima_data |> 
  rename(result = test) |> 
  mutate(result = as.factor(result))

set.seed(2026)

data_split <- initial_split(pima_data, prop = 0.80)
pima_train <- training(data_split)
pima_test  <- testing(data_split)



Part b (6 points)

Fit a logistic regression model to the training set for predicting result from the glucose and bmi variables.
Then use the classifier_plot() function in the math3190package (make sure you re-installed it!) to make a plot that shows the regions on the glucose-bmi-plane that are predicted to be 0 and 1. This shows how logistic regression divides the classes. Use the training set as the data input in this function.
Next, obtain the predictions for the test set with a probability cutoff of 0.5 to predict if the person has diabetes. Create a confusion matrix using the confusionMatrix() function in the caret library. Make sure to put positive = "1" for this and all other confusion matrices on this assignment. Print all of the output from that function that shows the confusion matrix and many associated statistics.
Write a sentence or two discussing how well this model performs in predicting on the test set.


logistic_model <- glm(result ~ glucose + bmi, data = pima_train, family = binomial)

classifier_plot(logistic_model, data = pima_train)







logistic_predictions <- predict(logistic_model, newdata = pima_test, type = "response")

confusionMatrix(
  data = factor(ifelse(logistic_predictions > 0.5, "1", "0"), levels = c("0", "1")), 
  reference = factor(pima_test$result, levels = c("0", "1")), 
  positive = "1"
)

Confusion Matrix and Statistics

          Reference
Prediction  0  1
         0 88 22
         1 13 31
                                          
               Accuracy : 0.7727          
                 95% CI : (0.6984, 0.8363)
    No Information Rate : 0.6558          
    P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 0.001115        
                                          
                  Kappa : 0.4754          
                                          
 Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 0.176296        
                                          
            Sensitivity : 0.5849          
            Specificity : 0.8713          
         Pos Pred Value : 0.7045          
         Neg Pred Value : 0.8000          
             Prevalence : 0.3442          
         Detection Rate : 0.2013          
   Detection Prevalence : 0.2857          
      Balanced Accuracy : 0.7281          
                                          
       'Positive' Class : 1               
                                          


This model does pretty well at predicting 0’s, but does not do as well with predicting 1’s as the model is predicting correctly about half the time for a result of 1.


Part c (3 points)
Now let’s use naïve Bayes to get predictions. Use the naive_bayes() function in the naivebayes library to fit a model predicting result using glucose and bmi. Find the predicted probabilities of being a 0 and a 1 for the first observation in the training set.

naivebayes_model <- naive_bayes(result ~ glucose + bmi, data = pima_train)
predict(naivebayes_model, newdata = pima_train[1, ], type = "prob")

              0         1
[1,] 0.07058255 0.9294174




Part d (9 points)
Now find the predicted probability of being a 0 and a 1 for the first observation in the training set “by hand” like we did in the notes on pages 187 and 188. To simplify this a bit, let’s again only use the variables glucose and bmi here. Of course, use R to help you (the dnorm() function and some tidyverse functions will be especially nice), but don’t use the naive_bayes() function here. Note: these variables we are using to predict are numeric, so we will assume a normal distribution for both of them (maybe not a good assumption, but let’s go with it). After you get the probabilities, compare them to the ones you got in part (c). They should match

library(tidyverse)

pima_train[1,]

    pregnant glucose diastolic triceps insulin  bmi diabetes age result
733        2     174        88      37     120 44.5    0.646  24      1

pima_summary <- pima_train |> 
  group_by(result) |> 
  summarise(
    n             = n(),
    mean_glucose  = mean(glucose),
    stdev_glucose = sd(glucose),
    mean_bmi      = mean(bmi),
    stdev_bmi     = sd(bmi)
  ) |>
  bind_rows(
    tibble(result = "Total", n = nrow(pima_train))
  )

pima_summary  

# A tibble: 3 × 6
  result     n mean_glucose stdev_glucose mean_bmi stdev_bmi
  <chr>  <int>        <dbl>         <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>
1 0        399         110.          26.2     30.1      7.69
2 1        215         139.          31.9     35.3      7.06
3 Total    614          NA           NA       NA       NA   



\[P(result = 0 | glucose = 174, bmi = 44.5) \propto P(result = 0) \cdot P(glucose = 174 | result = 0) \cdot P(bmi = 44.5 | result = 0)\]


p_result_0 <- pima_summary$n[pima_summary$result == "0"] / pima_summary$n[pima_summary$result == "Total"]

p_glucose_result_0 <- dnorm(174, pima_summary$mean_glucose[pima_summary$result == "0"], pima_summary$stdev_glucose[pima_summary$result == "0"])

p_bmi_result_0 <- dnorm(44.5, pima_summary$mean_bmi[pima_summary$result == "0"], pima_summary$stdev_bmi[pima_summary$result == "0"])

p_result_0 * p_glucose_result_0 * p_bmi_result_0

[1] 4.488662e-06



\[P(result = 1 | glucose = 174, bmi = 44.5) \propto P(result = 1) \cdot P(glucose = 174 | result = 1) \cdot P(bmi = 44.5 | result = 1)\]


p_result_1 <- pima_summary$n[pima_summary$result == "1"] / pima_summary$n[pima_summary$result == "Total"]

p_glucose_result_1 <- dnorm(174, pima_summary$mean_glucose[pima_summary$result == "1"], pima_summary$stdev_glucose[pima_summary$result == "1"])

p_bmi_result_1 <- dnorm(44.5, pima_summary$mean_bmi[pima_summary$result == "1"], pima_summary$stdev_bmi[pima_summary$result == "1"])

p_result_1 * p_glucose_result_1 * p_bmi_result_1

[1] 5.910584e-05



p_result_0_prob <- p_result_0 * p_glucose_result_0 * p_bmi_result_0 / (p_result_0 * p_glucose_result_0 * p_bmi_result_0 + p_result_1 * p_glucose_result_1 * p_bmi_result_1)

p_result_1_prob <- p_result_1 * p_glucose_result_1 * p_bmi_result_1 / (p_result_0 * p_glucose_result_0 * p_bmi_result_0 + p_result_1 * p_glucose_result_1 * p_bmi_result_1)

tibble(
  result    = c("0", "1"),
  prob = c(p_result_0_prob, p_result_1_prob)
)

# A tibble: 2 × 2
  result   prob
  <chr>   <dbl>
1 0      0.0706
2 1      0.929 


The probabilities came out the same as the ones found using the predict() function, as expected.


Part e (3 points)
Now fit a naïve Bayes model predicting result from glucose and bmi using a kernel instead of assuming a normal distribution. You can do this by setting usekernel = TRUE in the naive_bayes() function.

naivebayes_kernel_model <- naive_bayes(result ~ glucose + bmi, data = pima_train, usekernel = TRUE)



Part f (5 points)
Now use the classifier_plot() function to make two plots: one using the first naïve Bayes from part (c) and one using the naïve Bayes with the kernel from part (e). Both should show the separation on the training set. Comment on how these models seems to perform in separating the results classes and if there seems like there may be any over-fitting here.

classifier_plot(naivebayes_model, data = pima_train) + ggtitle("Base Naive Bayes")







classifier_plot(naivebayes_kernel_model, data = pima_train) + ggtitle("Naive Bayes with Kernel")








With how chaotic the boundary lines appear when using the model with the kernal, it seems like there may be some overfitting in that model. The base model has a much smoother boundary line, which indicates signs of better generalization.


Part g (5 points)
Report the output of the confusionMatrix() function for both naïve Bayes models when predicting on the test set. Write a couple sentences comparing the confusion matrix statistics.

naivebayes_model_predict <- predict(naivebayes_model, newdata = pima_test, type = "class")

confusionMatrix(naivebayes_model_predict, factor(pima_test$result, levels = c("0", "1")), positive = "1")

Confusion Matrix and Statistics

          Reference
Prediction  0  1
         0 89 21
         1 12 32
                                          
               Accuracy : 0.7857          
                 95% CI : (0.7124, 0.8477)
    No Information Rate : 0.6558          
    P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 0.0003074       
                                          
                  Kappa : 0.5054          
                                          
 Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 0.1637344       
                                          
            Sensitivity : 0.6038          
            Specificity : 0.8812          
         Pos Pred Value : 0.7273          
         Neg Pred Value : 0.8091          
             Prevalence : 0.3442          
         Detection Rate : 0.2078          
   Detection Prevalence : 0.2857          
      Balanced Accuracy : 0.7425          
                                          
       'Positive' Class : 1               
                                          

naivebayes_kernal_model_predict <- predict(naivebayes_kernel_model, newdata = pima_test, type = "class")

confusionMatrix(naivebayes_kernal_model_predict, factor(pima_test$result, levels = c("0", "1")), positive = "1")

Confusion Matrix and Statistics

          Reference
Prediction  0  1
         0 87 21
         1 14 32
                                          
               Accuracy : 0.7727          
                 95% CI : (0.6984, 0.8363)
    No Information Rate : 0.6558          
    P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 0.001115        
                                          
                  Kappa : 0.4802          
                                          
 Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 0.310494        
                                          
            Sensitivity : 0.6038          
            Specificity : 0.8614          
         Pos Pred Value : 0.6957          
         Neg Pred Value : 0.8056          
             Prevalence : 0.3442          
         Detection Rate : 0.2078          
   Detection Prevalence : 0.2987          
      Balanced Accuracy : 0.7326          
                                          
       'Positive' Class : 1               
                                          


The model that uses a kernal seems to be performing worse than the base model, as two instances that had previously been correctly classified as a 0 are being incorrectly classified as a 1. This is likely due to the overfitting that was observed in part (f).


Part h (3 points)
Now let’s use a support vector machine (SVM) for classifying diabetes using all other variables. We’ll use the svm() function in the e1071 library. Fit a model with a polynomial kernel here with degree 3 (the default), a cost of 1 (also the default), and keep scale = TRUE (which is the default as well). Ideally, we would do a cross validation to select the cost, but we’ll do that for the next kernel.

library(e1071)

svm_poly_3_model <- svm(result ~ glucose + bmi, data = pima_train, kernel = "polynomial", degree = 3, cost = 1, scale = TRUE)



Part i (10 points)
Now let’s use a different kernel. Let’s use the radial kernel for the SVM. This has two tuning parameters: the cost and \(\gamma\). Let’s use the train() function in the caret package to do 5-fold cross validation (set a seed, please) to select both of those parameters. However, the train() function uses \(\sigma\) instead of \(\gamma\) and \(C\) instead of cost.
Use the trainControl() function with the trControl option and the tuneGrid option in the train() function to perform this cross-validation. For sigma, use seq(0.1, 2, by = 0.1) and for C also use seq(0.1, 2, by = 0.1). Put method = "svmRadial" in the train() function as well so it knows to use a SVM with the radial kernel. You will need to install the kernlab package for this to work. Set a seed here as well.
By default, the “best” sigma and C values will be chosen with accuracy instead of Cohen’s kappa. Make sure to choose the best values using kappa (although both metrics may choose the same “best” values).

library(kernlab)

set.seed(2026)

cv_control <- trainControl(
  method = "cv",
  number = 5,
  selectionFunction = "best"
)

tune_grid <- expand.grid(
  sigma = seq(0.1, 2, by = 0.1),
  C     = seq(0.1, 2, by = 0.1)
)

svm_radial_cv <- train(
  result ~ .,
  data      = pima_train,
  method    = "svmRadial",
  trControl = cv_control,
  tuneGrid  = tune_grid,
  metric    = "Kappa"
)

svm_radial_cv$bestTune

  sigma   C
8   0.1 0.8




Part j (3 points)
Fit the SVM with the radial kernel using the svm() function and the cross-validated cost and \(\gamma\) found in part (i).

svm_radial_model <- svm(result ~ glucose + bmi, data = pima_train, kernel = "radial", cost = svm_radial_cv$bestTune$C, gamma = svm_radial_cv$bestTune$sigma, scale = TRUE)



Part k (5 points)
Create the classifier plots for both SVM models and plot the training set and comment on how well these seem to separate the classes.

classifier_plot(svm_poly_3_model, pima_train) + ggtitle("SVM with Polynomial Kernel")







classifier_plot(svm_radial_model, pima_train) + ggtitle("SVM with Radial Kernel")








It apears that the radial kernal svm is doing a better job at seperating the classes when compared to the polynomial kernal svm. The polynomial kernal svm is taking out area chunks in which there are no data points, which when combined with the new boundary trajectory makes it appear to be fitting the data worse.


Part l (3 points)
Report the confusion matrix statistics for both SVM models when predicting the test set.

poly_svm_predict <- predict(svm_poly_3_model, newdata = pima_test, type = "class")

confusionMatrix(poly_svm_predict, factor(pima_test$result, levels = c("0", "1")), positive = "1")

Confusion Matrix and Statistics

          Reference
Prediction  0  1
         0 97 35
         1  4 18
                                          
               Accuracy : 0.7468          
                 95% CI : (0.6705, 0.8133)
    No Information Rate : 0.6558          
    P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 0.009756        
                                          
                  Kappa : 0.3484          
                                          
 Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 1.556e-06       
                                          
            Sensitivity : 0.3396          
            Specificity : 0.9604          
         Pos Pred Value : 0.8182          
         Neg Pred Value : 0.7348          
             Prevalence : 0.3442          
         Detection Rate : 0.1169          
   Detection Prevalence : 0.1429          
      Balanced Accuracy : 0.6500          
                                          
       'Positive' Class : 1               
                                          

radial_svm_predict <- predict(svm_radial_model, newdata = pima_test, type = "class")

confusionMatrix(radial_svm_predict, factor(pima_test$result, levels = c("0", "1")), positive = "1")

Confusion Matrix and Statistics

          Reference
Prediction  0  1
         0 92 23
         1  9 30
                                          
               Accuracy : 0.7922          
                 95% CI : (0.7195, 0.8533)
    No Information Rate : 0.6558          
    P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 0.0001529       
                                          
                  Kappa : 0.5089          
                                          
 Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 0.0215563       
                                          
            Sensitivity : 0.5660          
            Specificity : 0.9109          
         Pos Pred Value : 0.7692          
         Neg Pred Value : 0.8000          
             Prevalence : 0.3442          
         Detection Rate : 0.1948          
   Detection Prevalence : 0.2532          
      Balanced Accuracy : 0.7385          
                                          
       'Positive' Class : 1               
                                          


While the polynomial svm does slightly better at predicting 0’s, radial still does a descent job at predicting 0’s while being much better at predicting 1’s.


Part m (10 points)
Compare the output of the confusionMarix() function for all of our classifiers: the logistic regression model, the naïve Bayes with a normal assumption, the naïve Bayes using kernel density estimation, the SVM with a polynomial kernel, and the SVM using a radial kernel. Which was best? Which was worst? How different were the results? You can focus on the Cohen’s \(\kappa\) and balanced accuracy for each one.

model_comparison <- tibble(
  Model = c("Logistic", "Naive Bayes", "Naive Bayes with Kernal", "Polynomial SVM", "Radial SVM"),
  Kappa = c(0.4754, 0.5054, 0.4802, 0.3484, 0.5089),
  Balanced_Accuracy = c(0.7281, 0.7425, 0.7326, 0.6500, 0.7385)
)

model_comparison

# A tibble: 5 × 3
  Model                   Kappa Balanced_Accuracy
  <chr>                   <dbl>             <dbl>
1 Logistic                0.475             0.728
2 Naive Bayes             0.505             0.742
3 Naive Bayes with Kernal 0.480             0.733
4 Polynomial SVM          0.348             0.65 
5 Radial SVM              0.509             0.738


We can see that out of the 5 models we used, the polynomial svm performed the worst with 0.3484 and 0.6500 respectively for the kappa and balanced accuracy. The models that performed the classification the best were the base naive bayes with 0.5054 and 0.7425 respectively, and the radial svm with 0.5089 and 0.7385 respectively. For the most part, all of the models had Kappa values between 0.45 and 0.50 and balanced accuracy values between 0.72 and 0.74, which are not big differences. The polynomial svm was the only model that had performance values outside of those ranges, and that difference was quite a bit more than within those ranges.

title: Math 3190 Homework
text: Welcome! This website is where I share the coursework I’ve completed for Math 3190: Fundamentals of Data Science at Southern Utah University, taught by Professor Rick Brown. Each homework assignment is rendered from a Quarto (.qmd) document, with code, output, and written answers all in one place.

title: Math 3190 Homework
text:
About the course
Math 3190 is a hands-on introduction to the tools and methods of data science. Across ten assignments, the course moves through data wrangling with the tidyverse, querying databases with SQL, resampling methods like bootstrapping, regression and regularization, dimension reduction, classification, and time series analysis.
All of the work on this site is done in R with Quarto, and version-controlled in Git — which means every assignment here reflects not just the statistics, but also the workflow of reproducible, collaborative data science.

title: Math 3190 Homework
text:
What you’ll find here

Homework — all ten assignments, each with the full write-up, code, and output.
About — a bit more about me, the course, and the tools I’ve been learning.
GitHub repo — the source .qmd files and everything that builds this site.

Thanks for stopping by!

title: About
text: Hi, I’m Melanie Jensen, a student at Southern Utah University. I just recently graduated with a double major in Economics and Data Science, with minors in Actuarial Mathematics, Computer Science and Finance. This site started as a place to collect my Math 3190 assignments — it’s also turning into a little portfolio of the data science tools I’ve been learning along the way.

title: About
text: Hi, I’m Melanie Jensen, a student at Southern Utah University. I just recently graduated with a double major in Economics and Data Science, with minors in Actuarial Mathematics, Computer Science and Finance. This site started as a place to collect my Math 3190 assignments — it’s also turning into a little portfolio of the data science tools I’ve been learning along the way.

title: About
text:
About the course
Math 3190: Fundamentals of Data Science is an undergraduate course at Southern Utah University, taught by Professor Rick Brown. The course covers the full data-science workflow, including:

Data wrangling and exploratory analysis with the tidyverse
Querying relational databases with SQL
Statistical inference and resampling methods (bootstrapping)
Linear regression, regularization (ridge, lasso), and model selection
Dimension reduction and principal components
Classification methods (Naive Bayes, SVM)
Time series analysis and forecasting

Everything is done in a reproducible way using Quarto and Git, with assignments turned in via GitHub.

title: About
text:
Skills & tools
Through the coursework collected on this site, I’ve gained hands-on experience with:

Languages: R (primary), a bit of Python (via reticulate), SQL
Reproducible documents: Quarto, R Markdown
Data wrangling & viz: tidyverse (dplyr, tidyr, ggplot2), lubridate, patchwork, GGally
Statistical modeling: linear and logistic regression, glmnet for ridge/lasso, caret for cross-validation, pls and umap for dimension reduction, faraway
Machine learning & classification: caret, rsample, e1071, kernlab, naivebayes
Time series: forecast, tsibble, tseries, itsmr
Databases: DBI, RSQLite, dbplyr
Version control & collaboration: Git, GitHub, Unix command line

title: Homework Assignments
text:
This page collects every homework assignment from the course. Click any card below to open the full write-up with code, output, and answers. You can also use the sidebar on the left to jump between assignments.


Homework 1
Due Jan 29, 2026 · Notes 1–3
Setting up the data science toolkit: navigating the Unix command line, version-controlling a project with Git and GitHub, wrangling data with the tidyverse and pipes, and writing a first reproducible Quarto document with R, LaTeX, and Python code chunks.


Homework 2
Due Feb 5, 2026 · Notes 4
Querying relational databases from R. Writing SQL (SELECT, WHERE, GROUP BY, joins) against a local SQLite database using DBI and RSQLite, and translating dplyr pipelines into SQL with dbplyr.


Homework 3
Due Feb 12, 2026 · Notes 5
Verification methods for classifiers and regressors — training/test splits, confusion matrices, sensitivity and specificity, \(k\)-nearest neighbors, picking \(k\), and \(k\)-fold cross-validation tuned through caret.


Homework 4
Due Feb 19, 2026 · Notes 6
Optimization for statistics. Implementing Newton’s method and gradient descent by hand, then using R’s optimize() and optim() to fit models via Maximum Likelihood Estimation.


Homework 5
Due Feb 26, 2026 · Notes 5 & 7 (Part 1)
Bootstrap resampling for inference paired with a linear-regression refresher: checking model diagnostics and using remedial measures like transformations, bootstrapped confidence intervals, and weighted least squares.


Homework 6
Due Mar 5, 2026 · Notes 7 (Part 2)
Advanced regression: ridge, LASSO, and elastic-net regularization via glmnet, the bias–variance trade-off, logistic-regression elastic nets, and Poisson / quasi-Poisson models for count data.


Homework 7
Due Mar 26, 2026 · Notes 8
Dimension reduction for modeling and visualization — principal component analysis (PCA), partial least squares regression with interpretable PLS surrogates, and the non-linear UMAP projection.


Homework 8
Due Apr 2, 2026 · Notes 9
Bayesian methods: conditional probability and Bayes’ theorem, deriving priors, likelihoods, and posteriors, and drawing from posteriors via Gibbs and Metropolis MCMC samplers (with diagnostics).


Homework 9
Due Apr 9, 2026 · Notes 10
More classification techniques — the Naïve Bayes classifier, Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), and hard- and soft-margin Support Vector Machines for both linear and non-linear decision boundaries.


Homework 10
Due Apr 16, 2026 · Notes 11
Time series analysis. Exploring autocorrelation (ACF/PACF), decomposing a series into trend, seasonality, and error, fitting MA, AR, ARMA, and ARIMA models, and producing forecasts with auto.arima().

title: MATH 3190 Homework 5
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_5 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.
Also, there is a package I created with many functions and data files we will be using for the remainder of this class. You can install this package instead of downloading and reading in the data files. To download the package, do the following in R:
library(devtools)
library(remotes)
library(math3190package)

title: MATH 3190 Homework 5
text:
Problem 1 (35 points)
We first covered bootstrapping in Notes 5, but we returned to it here. Let’s do a problem on bootstrapping that takes ideas from Notes 5 and Notes 7.
The amount of money spent on health care is an important issue for workers because many companies provide health insurance that only partial covers many medical procedures. The director of employee benefits at a midsize company wants to determine the amount spent on health care by the typical hourly worker in the company. A random sample of 25 workers is selected and the amounts they spent on their family’s health care needs during the past year can be found in the healthcare tibble in the math3190package.

healthcare <- healthcare


Part a (4 points)
Graph the data using both a boxplot and normal quantile plot (QQ plot) and use these to assess whether the population has a normal distribution.

ggplot(healthcare, aes(x = "", y = amount)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(title = "Boxplot of Healthcare Costs", y = "Cost")







ggplot(healthcare, aes(sample = amount)) + 
  geom_qq() +
  geom_qq_line() +
  labs(title = "QQ Plot of Healthcare Costs")








The healthcare amounts are not normally distributed. The boxplot shows that the data is skewed to the right, and the QQ plot is showing signs of a heavy right tail, which violates the normality assumption.


Part b (4 points)
Construct a 95% confidence interval for the mean cost per household using a t-interval. You can use the t.test() function for this. Interpret this confidence interval in context of the problem and comment on whether the t-procedure is appropriate to use here.

t.test(healthcare$amount, conf.level = 0.95)


    One Sample t-test

data:  healthcare$amount
t = 2.8884, df = 24, p-value = 0.008078
alternative hypothesis: true mean is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 163.5954 982.6446
sample estimates:
mean of x 
   573.12 


The t-test CI indicates that the average amount spend on healthcare is between $163.60 and $982.64. However, the t-procedure is unreliable to use here because the normality assumption is violated, as evidenced by the box plot and QQ plot. Having the skew in such a small sample means that the confidence level is underestimated (the actual coverage is less than 95%) and the interval itself is overestimated (too wide). Both occur simultaneously because the wide interval starts too far to the left.


Part c (7 points)
Now let’s use bootstrapping to estimate a 95% interval for the mean.
First, write a loop yourself that will do this. In the loop, you’ll need to sample from your data set with replacement, take the mean of that sample, and then store those means. Have the loop do this 10,000 times. Then you can obtain a 95% interval by using the quantile function in R. Specifically, you can type quantile(means_vector, c(0.025, 0.975)) (if you call your vector means_vector) to get a 95% interval based on the percentile method.

set.seed(2026)
means_vector <- c() 

for (i in 1:10000) {
  sample_data <- sample(healthcare$amount, replace = TRUE)
  means_vector[i] <- mean(sample_data)
}

quantile(means_vector, c(0.025, 0.975))

    2.5%    97.5% 
 259.112 1003.602 




Part d (5 points)
Now use the bootstrapping() function in the math3190package library to obtain a bootstrap object using 10,000 samples. Then use the boot_ci() function to obtain the 95% intervals. Report the Percentile interval and BCa interval. Compare your interval from part (c) to the Percentile interval computed here. They should be similar.

set.seed(2026)

boot_mean <- function(d, i) {
  return(mean(d[i]))
}
bootstrap <- bootstrapping(data = healthcare$amount, boot_mean, 10000)
boot_ci(bootstrap, conf = 0.95, type = "perc")

BOOTSTRAP CONFIDENCE INTERVAL CALCULATIONS
Based on 10000 bootstrap replicates

# A tibble: 1 × 4
  interval_type conf_level lower upper
  <chr>              <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1 percent             0.95  261.  997.

Calculations and Intervals on Original Scale

boot_ci(bootstrap, conf = 0.95, type = "bca")

BOOTSTRAP CONFIDENCE INTERVAL CALCULATIONS
Based on 10000 bootstrap replicates

# A tibble: 1 × 4
  interval_type conf_level lower upper
  <chr>              <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1 bca                 0.95  306. 1235.

Calculations and Intervals on Original Scale
Some BCa intervals may be unstable

tibble(
  method = c("Part_c", "Percentile", "BCa"),
  lower = c(quantile(means_vector, 0.025), 261, 306),
  upper = c(quantile(means_vector, 0.975), 997, 1235),
  width = upper - lower
)

# A tibble: 3 × 4
  method     lower upper width
  <chr>      <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1 Part_c      259. 1004.  744.
2 Percentile  261   997   736 
3 BCa         306  1235   929 


We can see that the intervals are all pretty similar. The lower bound ranges from 259 to 306 and the upper bound ranges from 997 to 1235. The percentile interval is closest to the interval found in part c, being a slightly skinnier interval. This means that the part c interval is predicting with 95% confidence that the true mean healthcare cost is between $259 and $1004, while the percentile interval is predicting with 95% confidence that the true mean healthcare cost is between $261 and $997. The BCa interval is a bit wider, predicting with 95% confidence that the true mean healthcare cost is between $306 and $1235.


Part e (3 points)
Write a sentence or two comparing the intervals you obtained in parts (c) and (d) to the t-interval you constructed in part (b).

tibble(
  method = c("t-test", "Part_c", "Percentile", "BCa"),
  lower = c(163.5954, quantile(means_vector, 0.025), 261, 306),
  upper = c(982.6446, quantile(means_vector, 0.975), 997, 1235),
  width = upper - lower
)

# A tibble: 4 × 4
  method     lower upper width
  <chr>      <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1 t-test      164.  983.  819.
2 Part_c      259. 1004.  744.
3 Percentile  261   997   736 
4 BCa         306  1235   929 


The t-test interval is wider than both the part c and percentile intervals, which confirms that the t-test interval is overestimating the margin of error due to the skew in the data. With the t-test starting much sooner than the bootstrapped intervals, it also confirms the shift in the interval due to bias from the skew.


Part g (4 points)
Use the boostrapping() function with at least 10,000 samples to obtain a 95% confidence interval based on BCa for the median.

set.seed(2026)
boot_med <- function(d, i) {
  return(median(d[i]))
}
bootstrap_med <- bootstrapping(data = healthcare$amount, boot_med, 10000)
boot_ci(bootstrap_med, conf = 0.95, type = "bca")

BOOTSTRAP CONFIDENCE INTERVAL CALCULATIONS
Based on 10000 bootstrap replicates

# A tibble: 1 × 4
  interval_type conf_level lower upper
  <chr>              <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1 bca                 0.95   208   326

Calculations and Intervals on Original Scale
Some BCa intervals may be unstable




Part h (5 points)
The IQR is a measure of spread commonly used with the median. Construct a 95% confidence interval based on BCa for the IQR using the bootstrapping() function with at least 10,000 samples and interpret the interval. Also plot the output of the bootstrapping() function and comment on whether the distribution looks close to normal.

set.seed(2026)
boot_iqr <- function(d, i) {
  return(IQR(d[i]))
}
bootstrap_iqr <- bootstrapping(data = healthcare$amount, boot_iqr, 10000)
boot_ci(bootstrap_iqr, conf = 0.95, type = "bca")

BOOTSTRAP CONFIDENCE INTERVAL CALCULATIONS
Based on 10000 bootstrap replicates

# A tibble: 1 × 4
  interval_type conf_level lower upper
  <chr>              <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1 bca                 0.95    46   330

Calculations and Intervals on Original Scale
Some BCa intervals may be unstable

iqr_plot_data <- tibble(replicates = as.vector(bootstrap_iqr$t))

ggplot(iqr_plot_data, aes(x = replicates)) + 
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "Bootstrap Distribution of IQR",
       x = "Interquartile Range (IQR)",
       y = "Frequency") +
  theme_minimal()








The bootstrapping distribution appears to be approximately normal, so long as you don’t look at the concentration of outliers on the right side of the distribution. Due to where the outliers fall, it does not appear to be totally normal, as those will skew the descriptive statistics of the distribution.


Part i (3 points)
In all of these bootstraps, what did we have to assume about the population in order for these intervals to be valid?
In order for these intervals to be valid, we must assume that the sample is representative of the population and that the observations are independent. Unlike the t-test, the bootstrap intervals do not follow the assumption that the population is normally distributed, so we do not need to assume normality for the bootstrap intervals to be valid.

title: MATH 3190 Homework 5
text:
Problem 2 (55 points)
Concrete is the most important material in civil engineering. The concrete compressive strength is an important attribute of the concrete and our goal is to predict the concrete compressive strength (in MPa) from the following variables:

Cement (in \(\text{kg}/\text{m}^3\))
Blast furnace slag (in \(\text{kg}/\text{m}^3\))
Fly ash (in \(\text{kg}/\text{m}^3\))
Water (in \(\text{kg}/\text{m}^3\))
Superplasticizer (in \(\text{kg}/\text{m}^3\))
Coarse aggregate (in \(\text{kg}/\text{m}^3\))
Fine aggregate (in \(\text{kg}/\text{m}^3\))
Age of concrete (in days)

This dataset came from the UCI ML Repository and can be found on the Math3190_Sp26 GitHub page along with a Readme file and in the Concrete_Data tibble in the math3190package R library.

Part a (2 points)
Change the names of the variables in Concrete_Data so they are shorter yet still descriptive. Name this something other than Concrete_Data so you don’t overwrite it.

data <- Concrete_Data |> 
  rename(
    "cement" = "Cement (component 1)(kg in a m^3 mixture)",            
    "blast_furnace_slag" = "Blast Furnace Slag (component 2)(kg in a m^3 mixture)",
    "fly_ash" = "Fly Ash (component 3)(kg in a m^3 mixture)",           
    "water" = "Water  (component 4)(kg in a m^3 mixture)",            
    "superplasticizer" = "Superplasticizer (component 5)(kg in a m^3 mixture)", 
    "course_agg" = "Coarse Aggregate  (component 6)(kg in a m^3 mixture)", 
    "fine_agg" = "Fine Aggregate (component 7)(kg in a m^3 mixture)",    
    "age" = "Age (day)",                                            
    "compress_strength" = "Concrete compressive strength(MPa, megapascals)" 
  )



Part b (5 points)
In the GGally library is the function ggpairs(), which makes a nice scatterplot matrix in the ggplot2 style. GGally should have already been installed when you installed math3190package, so you will just need to load it. Using ggpairs(), Create this scatterplot matrix for all of the variables in the dataset (they should all be plotted together in one plot).
Comment on the scatterplot matrix. Which variables seem to have a (at least somewhat) linear relationship with compressive strength?

library(GGally)

ggpairs(data)








From the ggpairs matrix, it appears that cement has the strongest linear relationship with compressive strength, with a correlation of 0.498 and the scatterplot showing a positive linear-approximate relationship. Superplasticizer is also showing signs of have a positive linear relationship with compressive strength, with a correlation of 0.366, but showing less of a linear relationship in the scatterplot than what we see with cement. While the correlation for age is also relatively higher, at 0.329, the scatterplot is showing a concave shape, which is not linear.


Part c (3 points)
Fit a linear model (with the lm() function) predicting compressive strength using all other variables. After you’ve done that, interpret the slope of the water variable in the context of the problem and answer whether it is considered a significant variable in the model.

mod_lm <- lm(compress_strength ~ ., data = data)
summary(mod_lm)


Call:
lm(formula = compress_strength ~ ., data = data)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-28.653  -6.303   0.704   6.562  34.446 

Coefficients:
                     Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)        -23.163756  26.588421  -0.871 0.383851    
cement               0.119785   0.008489  14.110  < 2e-16 ***
blast_furnace_slag   0.103847   0.010136  10.245  < 2e-16 ***
fly_ash              0.087943   0.012585   6.988 5.03e-12 ***
water               -0.150298   0.040179  -3.741 0.000194 ***
superplasticizer     0.290687   0.093460   3.110 0.001921 ** 
course_agg           0.018030   0.009394   1.919 0.055227 .  
fine_agg             0.020154   0.010703   1.883 0.059968 .  
age                  0.114226   0.005427  21.046  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 10.4 on 1021 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6155,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.6125 
F-statistic: 204.3 on 8 and 1021 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16


An increase of 1 kg/m^3 of water is associated with a decrease in the predicted compressive strength of 0.150298 MPa, holding all other variables constant. The p-value for the water variable is 0.000194, which is less than the common significance level of 0.05, so we would consider water to be a significant variable in the model.


Part d (6 points)
Using ggplot(), make a QQ plot of the raw residuals (obtained using the residuals() function). Include the QQ line as well. Comment on whether the residuals appear to be approximately normal.

ggplot(data, aes(sample = residuals(mod_lm))) + 
  geom_qq() +
  geom_qq_line() +
  labs(title = "QQ Plot of Raw Residuals") + 
  theme_minimal()








The residuals appear to be approximately normal except at the lower end of the range (and two points at the upper end of the range), which is showing signs of being short tailed. This is not ideal, but it is not a huge violation of normality.
Using ggplot(), make a plot of the jackknife residuals (obtained using the rstudent() function) on the y-axis and the fitted values of the model on the x-axis. Comment on whether this residual plot looks good. If it does not, indicate what the problem(s) is (are).

ggplot(data, aes(x = fitted(mod_lm), y = rstudent(mod_lm))) + 
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", formula = y~x, se = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Jackknife Residuals vs Fitted Values",
       x = "Fitted Values",
       y = "Jackknife Residuals") +
  theme_minimal()








The residual plot is showing signs of heteroskedasticity, as not only are the redisuals fanning out as the fitted values increase, but there is also a clear concentration of points between fitted values of 20 and 40. This is a problem because it indicates that the assumption of constant variance and the assumption of uniformly distributed errors are violated.


Part e (6 points)
Since the residual plots do not look good, let’s try to use weighted least squares. Following the code on page 118 and 119 of Notes 7, create a vector of weights and then fit a new model using weighted least squares.

abs_resids <- abs(mod_lm$residuals)
fitted <- mod_lm$fitted.values
emod <- lm(abs_resids ~ fitted)
w <- 1/emod$fitted.values^2
mod_wls <- lm(compress_strength ~ ., data = data, weights = w)
summary(mod_wls)


Call:
lm(formula = compress_strength ~ ., data = data, weights = w)

Weighted Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-3.5154 -0.8319  0.0884  0.8213  4.5462 

Coefficients:
                     Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)        -32.403699  24.543797  -1.320  0.18705    
cement               0.123304   0.007963  15.484  < 2e-16 ***
blast_furnace_slag   0.102893   0.009346  11.010  < 2e-16 ***
fly_ash              0.081794   0.011420   7.162 1.52e-12 ***
water               -0.110369   0.036820  -2.998  0.00279 ** 
superplasticizer     0.441258   0.092782   4.756 2.26e-06 ***
course_agg           0.017182   0.008628   1.991  0.04669 *  
fine_agg             0.020097   0.009886   2.033  0.04233 *  
age                  0.146670   0.006189  23.699  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 1.208 on 1021 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6547,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.652 
F-statistic:   242 on 8 and 1021 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16




Part f (5 points)
Using ggplot(), make a plot of the jackknife residuals (obtained using the rstudent() function) on the y-axis and the fitted values of the weighted model on the x-axis. Comment on whether this residual plot looks good and whether this plot looks better than the residual plot in part d.

ggplot(data, aes(sample = rstudent(mod_wls))) + 
  geom_qq() +
  geom_qq_line() +
  labs(title = "QQ Plot of Jackknife Residuals (WLS)") + 
  theme_minimal()









ggplot(data, aes(x = fitted(mod_wls), y = rstudent(mod_wls))) + 
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", formula = y~x, se = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Jackknife Residuals vs Fitted Values (WLS)",
       x = "Fitted Values",
       y = "Jackknife Residuals") +
  theme_minimal()








The QQ plot using the weighted least squares model is showing better signs of normality, but the normality assumption is still in question for the lower- and upper-most ends of the distribution, as it is now showing signs of being slightly skewed to the right. The residual plot is showing much better as the fanning has turned into a more consistant spread of residuals across the range of fitted values, more so up to fitted values of around 60, where the residuals tend to be negative more often. While there is still a slight concentration of points between fitted values of 20 and 40, it is not as bad as what we saw in the unweighted model. Overall, the residual plots for the weighted model are showing better signs of meeting the assumptions than the residual plots for the unweighted model.


Part f (8 points)
Using the unweighted model from part c and the weighted model from part e, find and report both a confidence interval for the mean compressive strength and a prediction interval for the specific compressive strength for concrete that has a cement value of 300, a blast furnace slag of 90, a fly ash of 50, a water value of 200, a superplasticizer of 2.5, a coarse aggregate of 900, a fine aggregate of 600, and an age of 300 days. You can use the predict() function here and remember that you will need to find the specific weight for the given predictor variable values for the weighted intervals. You can follow along with the code used on page 121 of Notes 7.
Comment on how these intervals differ. Does the change make sense given the value of the fitted value?

new_obs <- data.frame(cement = 300, blast_furnace_slag = 90, fly_ash = 50, water = 200, 
                           superplasticizer = 2.5, course_agg = 900, fine_agg = 600, age = 300)

predict(mod_lm, new_obs, interval = "confidence")

       fit      lwr      upr
1 59.76989 55.66444 63.87534

predict(mod_wls, new_obs, interval = "confidence")

       fit      lwr      upr
1 68.49046 64.15589 72.82503

predict(mod_lm, new_obs, interval = "prediction")

       fit      lwr      upr
1 59.76989 38.95351 80.58627

new_fit <- predict(mod_lm, new_obs)
new_weight <- 1/predict(emod, data.frame(fitted = new_fit))^2
predict(mod_wls, new_obs, interval = "prediction", weights = new_weight)

       fit     lwr      upr
1 68.49046 39.9145 97.06641


The OLS model predicts with 95% confidence that for all possible samples with the provided parameters, the true population average compresseion strength falls between 55.66444 - 63.87534 MPa. With the OLS model, if a new concrete sample is made with the provided parameters, it predicts with 95% confidence that the compressive strength of that particular sample will fall between 39.9145 - 97.00641 MPa.
The WLS model predicts with 95% confidence that for all possible samples with the provided parameters, the true population average compresseion strength falls between 64.15589 - 72.82503 MPa. With the WLS model, if a new concrete sample is made with the provided parameters, it predicts with 95% confidence that the compressive strength of that particular sample will fall between 39.9145 - 97.06641 MPa.
We can see that both models have a wider prediction interval than their respective confidence intervals, which makes sense because the prediction interval is accounting for individual variability in the response variable, while the confidence interval is only accounting for variability in the mean variability in the response variable.
We can see that the confidence interval for the WLS model is shifted to the right and is narrower than the confidence interval for the OLS model. Despite this shift, the prediction intervals for both models are pretty similar, with the WLS model having a slightly wider prediction interval. This makes sense because the fitted value for the WLS model is higher than the fitted value for the OLS model, which would shift the confidence interval to the right. The prediction intervals are similar because they are both accounting for individual variability in the response variable, which is not affected by the weighting in the WLS model.


Part g (10 points)
Write a function called predict_weighted that takes three inputs: the unweighted model, a data frame containing information about the value(s) of the predictor variables we are using to predict, and the interval type (either “confidence” or “prediction”). This function should return the predicted value and the interval bounds for the specified interval type for weighted least squares. So, this function should compute the weights, obtain the weighted least squares model, find the specific weight for the new value, and then get the prediction and the interval. This function should work for any linear model (made with the lm() function) you give it, not just for this exact situation of this problem. So, you should not reference any data sets or variables specific to this concrete problem in the function.
Test this new function for the confidence and prediction intervals you made in part f. 

predict_weighted <- function(unweighted_model, new_data, interval_type) {
  abs_resids <- abs(mod_lm$residuals)
  fitted <- mod_lm$fitted.values
  emod <- lm(abs_resids ~ fitted)
  w <- 1/emod$fitted.values^2
  mod_wls <- lm(formula(unweighted_model), data = model.frame(unweighted_model), weights = w)

  new_fit <- predict(unweighted_model, new_data)
  new_weight <- 1/predict(emod, data.frame(fitted = new_fit))^2

  if (interval_type == "confidence") {
    return(predict(mod_wls, new_data, interval = "confidence"))
  } else if (interval_type == "prediction") {
    return(predict(mod_wls, new_data, interval = "prediction", weights = new_weight))
  } else {
    return("Invalid interval type. Please choose either 'confidence' or 'prediction'.")
  }
}

Across all three models, the bootstrapped standard errors are consistently the largest. The OLS (unweighted) model generally yields the second-highest errors, with the exception of the age variable. This inflation in the bootstrapped errors is expected because the bootstrap captures the true variability and the “noise” caused by heteroskedasticity that the OLS and WLS formulas ignore. Regarding the coefficient estimates, the values remain relatively stable across all models for most variables. However, the superplasticizer and age variables show a noticeable shift in the WLS model compared to the OLS and bootstrapped results. This divergence occurs because WLS applies weights to minimize the influence of high-variance observations. If the relationship between these specific predictors and the response variable is different for the “more reliable” (low-variance) observations, the WLS estimates will naturally shift to reflect that subset of the data more heavily.

title: MATH 3190 Homework 6
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_6 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.

Problem 1 (28 points)
An automobile consulting company wants to understand the factors on which the pricing of cars depends. The dataset car_price_prediction in the math3190package (and also found in the GitHub data folder) has information on the sales of 4340 vehicles.

Part a (3 points)
Create new variables in the dataset by taking the natural log of selling price, year, and km_driven. This will help linearize the association between variables. Then fit a linear model for predicting the log of selling price using all other variables except “name”, “year”, “selling_price”, and “km_driven” (the logged variables should be used instread of those last three). Also, there is only one vehicle with a fuel type of “Electric”. Remove that vehicle from the dataset.

cars <- car_price_prediction |> 
  mutate(
    log_selling_price = log(selling_price),
    log_year = log(year),
    log_km_driven = log(km_driven)
    ) |> 
  filter(fuel != "Electric") 

mod1lm <- lm(log_selling_price ~ . - name - year - selling_price - km_driven, data = cars)
summary(mod1lm)

Part b (5 points)
Now fit some LASSO models for predicting log selling price using all but the “name” variable. Try the following values for the regularization parameter: \(\lambda=0,\ 0.01,\ 0.1,\) and \(1\) and comment on how the coefficients of the model change.
Note: when \(\lambda=0\), the LASSO coefficients should equal the OLS model coefficients. However, they will actually be a bit off here. That is because the glmnet() function has a thresh argument that sets a threshold for convergence. It is, by default, set to 1e-07. To make the parameters match, you can change that to thresh = 1e-14 instead. This is not necessary, though.

mod1lasso <- glmnet(log_selling_price ~ . - name, data = cars, alpha = 1)
lambda <- c(0, 0.01, 0.1, 1)
results_list <- list()

for (l in lambda) {
  cat("\n--- Lambda:", l, "---\n")
  print(coef(mod1lasso, s = l))

  column_name <- paste0("lambda_", l)
  results_list[[column_name]] <- as.matrix(coef(mod1lasso, s = l))[,1]
}

  coef_columns[[column_name]] <- as.matrix(coef(mod1cv[[i]]))[,1]

  cat("\n--- Alpha Results:", a, "---\n")

}

Part f (8 points)
Use bootstrapping with at least 1000 samples to estimate the standard errors of the coefficients of the ridge regression model. For each bootstrap sample, run the cv.glmnet() function to find the best \(\lambda\) and fit a model using that optimized \(\lambda\). Use the “lambda.1se” for this. This can take a few minutes to run. Then compare these bootstrapped standard errors to the standard errors for the OLS model you fit in part a. Are they larger or smaller? Does this make sense?

set.seed(2026)
nboot <- 1000
coefs <- matrix(rep(0, nboot*13), nrow = nboot)
for (i in 1:nboot) {
  index <- sample(1:nrow(cars), nrow(cars), replace = T)
  mod <- cv.glmnet(log_selling_price ~ . - name, data = cars[index,], alpha = 0)
  coefs[i,] <- coef(mod, s = "lambda.min")[1:13]
}
boot_se <- apply(coefs, 2, sd)

st_error <- tibble(
  Variable = rownames(summary(mod1lm)$coefficients),
  OLS = summary(mod1lm)$coefficients[, "Std. Error"],
  Bootstrap = boot_se)

Answer: The standard errors with the bootstrapping method almost always come out significantly lower than that in the ols model. This makes sense because the bootstrapping is using the ridge regression amd optimized lambdas to minimize a penalized sum of squared errors.

Problem 3 (41 points)
In the AER package is the data set “ShipAccidents”. You can install that package and load that data using data(ShipAccidents). Type ?ShipAccidents to read about that data set.

Part a (4 points)
Load in the data, remove the rows for which service is equal to 0, and then fit a Poisson regression model for predicting incidents from all other variables using the ShipAccidents data. Don’t forget to put family = "poisson" in the glm() function. Briefly explain why it makes sense to do Poisson regression here.

data(ShipAccidents)
ship_accidents <- ShipAccidents |> 
  filter(service != 0)

mod3poisson <- glm(incidents ~ ., data = ship_accidents, family = "poisson")
summary(mod3poisson)

Answer: A poisson distribution is appropriate here because we are predicting the number of ship incidents, which is a discrete quantitative variable.


Part b (5 points)
Using that model, interpret the slope of “service” and the slope of “construction1970-74” in original (not log) units.

exp(summary(mod3poisson)$coefficients['service',][1])

Estimate 
1.000064 

exp(summary(mod3poisson)$coefficients['construction1970-74',][1])

Estimate 
4.247106 


Answer: As the number of months of service increases by 1 month, the average number of ship incidents is predicted to increase by a factor of 1.000064, or by 0.0064%, keeping all other variables constant. Ships built between 1970-1974 are predicted to have 4.246 times the average number of incidents compared to ships built in 1960-1964, holding all other variables constant.


Part c (6 points)
Make a residual plot of the Pearson residuals vs the linear predictors and make a QQ plot of the Pearson residuals. Comment on what these imply about the fit.

p_resids <- residuals(mod3poisson, type = "pearson")
resid_df <- data.frame(p_resids, linear_pred = mod3poisson$linear.predictors)

ggplot(resid_df, aes(x = linear_pred, y = p_resids)) + 
  geom_point(size = 0.5) + 
  labs(x = "Linear Predictors", y = "Pearson Residuals") + 
  theme_minimal()







ggplot(resid_df, aes(sample = p_resids)) +
  geom_qq() + 
  geom_qq_line(linewidth = 1) + 
  labs(x = "Theoretical Quantiles", y = "Sample Quantiles") +
  theme_minimal()








Answer: We can see in the residual plot that the assumption random spread is met, as there does not appear to be any significant patterns occuring in the data. However, we see in the QQ Plot that the distribution of pearson redisuals is skewed to the right, which violates the assumption that the residuals are approximately normal.


Part d (5 points)
Conduct a deviance goodness-of-fit (GOF) test for a lack of fit here. Type out the null and alternative hypotheses, report the test statistic, give the p-value, and provide an interpretation.

print(mod3poisson$deviance)

[1] 77.44977

print(mod3poisson$df.residual)

[1] 24

1 - pchisq(mod3poisson$deviance, df = mod3poisson$df.residual)

[1] 1.542447e-07

mod3poisson$deviance/mod3poisson$df.residual

[1] 3.227074


Answer: \(H_0\): The model is correctly specified \(H_1\): The model is incorrectly specified Test Statistic: \(77.44977\) Degrees of Freedom: p-value: \(1.542447e-07\)
The GOF test statistic is approximately \(3\) times the size of the degrees of freedom, which indicates that there is more variation in the number of ship incidents than the poisson model can account for. This is backed up by the p-value, which is signifiantly smaller than 0.05 (reject the null hypothesis) indicating that there is overwhelming evidence that this poisson model is a poor fit for this data.


Part e (4 points)
Is there evidence that this model has overdispersion? Explain.

phi <- sum(residuals(mod3poisson, type = "pearson")^2)/mod3poisson$df.residual
phi

[1] 2.896271


Answer: Overdispersion is when the variance of a model is greater than its mean, which in this case violates the fundamental property of the poisson distribution which assumes that the variance is equal to the mean. Because the sum of squared pearson residuals divided by the degrees of freedom (the dispersion) is greater than 1.25, there is evidence of that this model has overdispersion and correction for this is warranted.


Part f (6 points)
Fit a “quasipoisson” model to these data. Using this model, obtain an approximate 95% confidence interval for the mean response for a ship of type “B” with construction between 1965 and 1969, with operation between 1960 and 1974, and with 2000 aggregate months of service. You can construct this interval using a t critical value with 24 degrees of freedom since the quasipoisson family more closely follows a t distribution than a normal one. Interpret the interval.

mod3quasipoisson <- glm(incidents ~ ., data = ship_accidents, family = "quasipoisson")
summary(mod3quasipoisson)

incident_pred_quasi <- predict(mod3quasipoisson, data.frame(type = "B", construction = "1965-69", operation = "1960-74", service = 2000), se.fit = T)

t_crit = qt(0.975, df = mod3quasipoisson$df.residual)

  Lower = exp(incident_pred_quasi$fit - t_crit * incident_pred_quasi$se.fit),
  Upper = exp(incident_pred_quasi$fit + t_crit * incident_pred_quasi$se.fit)
)

  Lower Upper
  <dbl> <dbl>
1  3.66  19.9


Answer: We are 95% confident that the average number of incidents a ship of type B that was constructed between 1965 and 1969, was in operation between 1960 and 1974 and was in service for 2000 months had is between 3.66 and 19.9 incidents.


Part g (5 points)
Now construct an approximate 95% prediction interval for the number of incidents for the ship described in part f using the qpois() function. Interpret the interval.

predicted_mean_quasi <- exp(incident_pred_quasi$fit)

tibble(
  lower = qpois(0.025, lambda = predicted_mean_quasi),
  upper = qpois(0.975, lambda = predicted_mean_quasi)
)

# A tibble: 1 × 2
  lower upper
  <dbl> <dbl>
1     3    15


Answer: We are 95% confident that the actual number of incidents a ship of type B that was constructed between 1965 and 1969, was in operation between 1960 and 1974 and was in service for 2000 months had is between 3 and 15 incidents.


Part h (6 points)
Suppose we did not catch the fact that this model is overdispersed. Repeat parts f and g using the model fit with the “poisson” family, not “quasipoisson” and using a z interval for the mean response rather than a t interval. Compare your results to parts f and g and comment on what is different.

incident_pred_poisson <- predict(mod3poisson, data.frame(type = "B", construction = "1965-69", operation = "1960-74", service = 2000), se.fit = T)

z_crit = 1.96

tibble(
  Lower = exp(incident_pred_poisson$fit - z_crit * incident_pred_poisson$se.fit),
  Upper = exp(incident_pred_poisson$fit + z_crit * incident_pred_poisson$se.fit)
)

# A tibble: 1 × 2
  Lower Upper
  <dbl> <dbl>
1  5.33  13.7

predicted_mean_poisson <- exp(incident_pred_poisson$fit)

tibble(
  lower = qpois(0.025, lambda = predicted_mean_poisson),
  upper = qpois(0.975, lambda = predicted_mean_poisson)
)

# A tibble: 1 × 2
  lower upper
  <dbl> <dbl>
1     3    15


Answer: We can see that using the poisson model and a z-distribution creates a much narrower confidence interval compared to what was calculated using the quasi-poisson model and a t-distribution. This makes sense because this interval is for the average incidents within the specific charactoristic, and the quasi-poisson model is accounting for the overdispersion where the regular poisson distribution is not. We can see that the qpois() confidence intervals are resulting in the same confidence interval, regardless on when the poisson or quasi-poisson models are used. This makes sense because this confidence interval is for the actual incidence a specific ship may have, rather than an average across similar ships.

title: MATH 3190 Homework 3
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_3 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.

Problem 1 (11 points)

Part a (9 points)
Suppose we are attempting to predict a person’s ability to run a marathon in under 4 hours (coded as a 1) based on a number of factors: age, sex, BMI, and blood pressure. Below is the confusion matrix in this situation:




Actual: 1
Actual: 0




Predicted: 1
58
102


Predicted: 0
37
217



Find each of the following. Use proper formatting in Quarto when you type your answers. You can put equations between dollar signs ($$) and you can use the \frac{}{} (for a small fraction) or \dfrac{}{} (for a larger one) commands to nicely type fractions.

The prevalence of those that can run a mile under 4 hours.

\[Prevalence = \frac{58 + 37}{58 + 102 + 37 + 217} = 0.2294686\]

The overall accuracy of these predictions.

\[Overall Accuracy = \frac{58 + 217}{58 + 102 + 37 + 217} = 0.6642512\]

The sensitivity (recall).

\[Sensitivity = \frac{58}{58 + 37} = 0.6105263\]

The specificity.

\[Specificity = \frac{217}{217 + 102} = 0.6802508\]

The positive predictive value (precision).

\[Precision = \frac{58}{58 + 102} = 0.3625\]

The negative predictive value.

\[Negative Predictive Value = \frac{217}{217 + 37} = 0.8543307\]

The balanced accuracy.

\[Balanced Accuracy = \frac{\frac{58}{58 + 37} + \frac{217}{217 + 102}}{2} = 0.6453885\]

Cohen’s Kappa (\(\kappa\)). Check out this link on Wikipedia: Cohen’s Kappa and scroll down to the section entitled Binary classification confusion matrix.

\[$\kappa$ = \frac{2 * (58 * 217 - 37 * 102)}{(58 + 102) * (102 + 217) + (58 + 37) * (37 + 217)} = 0.2344552\]

F1 score. Check out what this is on Wikipedia: F-Score

\[F1 Score = \frac{2 * 58}{2 * 58 + 102 + 37} = 0.454902\]


Part b (2 points)
Read more of the Wikipedia article on Cohen’s Kappa, especially the Interpreting magnitude and the Limitations part. I cannot really verify that you did this, so this is on your honor.



Problem 2 (79 points)
The adult dataset (from the UC Irvine database) is one that is used to predict whether a person makes over $50K a year based on some other variables. The data came from the Census Bureau in 1994 and can be found in the Data folder in my Math3190_S26 GitHub repo. More info on the dataset can be found in the “adult.names” file.

Part a (4 points)
Read the data into R as a tibble, change the column names to be descriptive about what the variable in that column is, and change the one containing salary information to a factor. Read the “adult.names” file to see the column names. Note that even though this file has a .data. suffix, it is comma separated, so you can read it in using read.csv() in base R and the convert it to a tibble or read_csv() from the tidyverse, which wil read it in as a tibble.

Fit two models for predicting whether a person’s salary is above $50K or not:

By the way, if you are unfamiliar with logistic regression, take some time to research it online or in my notes from Math 3150, which you can find here. We will deal with logistic regression again on the next homework. You don’t need to know everything about it, just learn a little about how it is used to predict the class for an observation. Of course, I’m happy to explain how it works in office hours as well.
For the second model, fit a \(k\) nearest neighbors model with \(k=7\) neighbors using the knn3() function in the caret package. Again, use age, education, race, sex, and hours_per_week as the predictors.

Part h (20 points)
We mentioned the most common cutoff value for the predicted probability for predicting a “success” in logistic regression is 0.5. However, we can adjust this value to make it easier or more difficult to predict a success. Let’s optimize this cutoff value using 5-fold cross validation. Note: we could also do this with kNN, but we will not on this assignment.
Using the cutoff values from 0.15 to 0.85 by 0.05 (0.15, 0.20, 0.25, and so on up to 0.85) for predicting whether an adult has a salary above 50K, find which one performs best on the training set using the metric of Cohen’s \(\kappa\), which is given in the output of the confusionMatrix() function. You will need a couple loops here since the train() function cannot do this for us. Note: you can find the indices of the rows in each fold using the createFolds() function in the caret library. Please set a seed at the beginning of your code chunk for this part.

set.seed(2026)

folds <- createFolds(adults_train$salary, k = 5)
cutoffs <- seq(0.15, 0.85, by = 0.05)
kappa_results <- matrix(NA, nrow = 5, ncol = length(cutoffs))
colnames(kappa_results) <- cutoffs

for(i in 1:5) {
  cv_train <- adults_train[-folds[[i]], ]
  cv_val   <- adults_train[folds[[i]], ]
  
  mod <- glm(salary ~ age + education + race + sex + hours_per_week, 
             data = cv_train, family = "binomial")
  
  probs <- predict(mod, cv_val, type = "response")
  
  for(j in 1:length(cutoffs)) {
    preds <- ifelse(probs >= cutoffs[j], ">50K", "<=50K")
    preds <- factor(preds, levels = c("<=50K", ">50K"))
    
    cm <- confusionMatrix(preds, cv_val$salary)
    kappa_results[i, j] <- cm$overall["Kappa"]
  }
}

mean_kappas <- colMeans(kappa_results, na.rm = TRUE)
best_cutoff <- cutoffs[which.max(mean_kappas)]

print(paste("The optimal cutoff based on Mean Kappa is:", best_cutoff))

However, the logistic model had better metrics in specificity and negative predicted values.
Even though one method may be better on a given dataset than another, that does not mean that method will always predict better. However, logistic regression has a few advantages over kNN regardless of predictive power. List at least three advantages logistic regression has over kNN.
Three advantages that logistic regression has over KNN include having better computational power (code takes longer to run on large datasets as it is computationally less complex), distinct probabilities in classifications (compared to a nearest neightbor, where the neighbor is arbitrary), and the logistic model being less sensitive to outliers (only impacts the average liklihoods rather than dominating a neighbor).
